chunks.text: terms. The success of this approach is dictated by the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) Complementary condition [6] at the optimal solution ˆβ∗: ˆβ∗ k · g∗ k = 0 ∀k (23) where g∗ k is the objective gradient. This condition mandates a strict bifurcation of the overcomplete dictionary. If a feature is inactive (spurious or collinear), it is pinned to the boundary ( ˆβ∗ k = 0) with a positive gradient pointing outward. If a feature is active ( ˆβ∗ k > 0), its local gradient must be exactly zero (g∗ k = 0). Once the optimal scaled vector ˆβ∗is identified via the KKTconstrained active-set projection, the exact physical stiffness parameters are recovered via the inverse mapping: β∗= syS−1 ˆβ∗ (24) Because this scaling is a purely linear transformation and avoids objective-function penalties (e.g., λ∥β∥1), it preserves the exact unbiased local minimum. This "Dual-Manifold" approach combines the numerical stability of bounded optimization with the theoretical exactitude of Ordinary Least Squares strictly on the recovered physical support manifold. A standard numerical threshold filter is applied post-recovery to eliminate machineprecision floating-point artifacts, yielding exact L0 sparsity. Since the gradient is precisely zero for all active terms, the NNLS active-set algorithm operates equivalent to unconstrained Ordinary Least Squares strictly on the true physical support manifold. This isolates the L0 unpenalized minimum, recovering the exact theoretical amplitude (e.g., retrieving a stiffness of exactly 250.0) while enforcing global convexity and unconditional thermodynamic stability. The SINP basis library employed here captures the general topology of the learned potentials but sometimes struggles with complex cross-coupling terms in the most nonlinear cases; future work will expand the library to include physically motivated functional forms from continuum damage mechanics (e.g., (1 − α)nε2 coupling terms) [13, 14, 57]. TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks Supplementary Material Rheological representations Phenomenologically, the overall mechanical behavior of any materials, in 1D, can be modeled by arbitrary serial and parallel arrangements of a few elementary (non)-linear constitutive model elements elastic springs, viscous dashpots, and frictional sliders, representing the elastic, viscous, and plastic processes, respectively [87]. Figure S1 shows the rheological representations for Maxwell and Burgers viscoelastic models. The formulas of Helmholtz free energy for Maxwell and Burgers models are Ψ = 2 Evϵ2 e and Ψ = 2 Ev1ϵ2 e1 + 2 Ev2ϵ2 e2, respectively. The formulas of dual dissipation for Maxwell and Burgers models are Φ∗= 2ηv σ2 v and Φ∗= 2ηv1 σ2 v1 + 2ηv2 σ2 v2, respectively. 𝑬𝒗 𝜼𝒗 𝑬𝒗𝟏 𝑬𝒗𝟐 𝜼𝒗𝟐 𝜼𝒗𝟏 Maxwell Burgers Fig. S1. Rheological representations of the Maxwell viscoelastic model with a single internal variable (associated with ηv), and Burgers viscoelastic model with two internal variables (associated with ηv1 and ηv2 ). TIP-INN training details In all test cases, the learning rate was set to 10−3, and the training was allowed to progress for 20,000 epochs with an early stopping criterion to avoid overfitting. In TIP-INN, the convex neural network architecture consists of an input layer with a linear activation function, followed by a single hidden layer. The hidden layer contains multiple fully connected neurons for the Maxwell and Burgers models, and 20 fully connected neurons for the experimental datasets, and is used to learn the Helmholtz free energy (Ψ) and dual dissipation Φ∗. The output layer with a linear activation function contains a single neuron, consistent with the scalar nature of Ψ and Φ∗. As described

chunks.title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
chunks.text: Maxwell and Burgers models, and 20 fully connected neurons for the experimental datasets, and is used to learn the Helmholtz free energy (Ψ) and dual dissipation Φ∗. The output layer with a linear activation function contains a single neuron, consistent with the scalar nature of Ψ and Φ∗. As described in Section 6, a quadratic activation function is used for modeling Maxwell and Burgers viscoelastic materials, while a smoothed absolute activation function is employed for experimental data. Our numerical tests indicate that a single hidden layer with 10-30 neurons is generally sufficient to capture a wide range of constitutive behaviors from both analytical and experimental datasets. Increasing the hidden layer depth does not improve the accuracy of the predicted thermodynamic potentials. In fact, introducing multiple hidden layers (e.g., > 4) can lead to training instability, as the quadratic activation function amplifies weights multiplicatively across layers, resulting in divergence. TIP-INN to model Maxwell viscoelastic materials Figure S2 shows the predicted stress for different types of loads by training TIP-INN with a single load. Notice that accurate stresses were predicted in nearly all cases. Figure S3 presents the predicted stress and Helmholtz free energy obtained by TIP-INN on the training dataset for the Maxwell viscoelastic model. The results are consistent with those observed for the testing data discussed in Section 3.1, leading to similar conclusions regarding the accuracy and robustness of TIP-INN. In addition, Figure S3 shows that the training and testing datasets exhibit distinct frequency characteristics, as revealed by the power spectral analysis. In this study, the Lomb-Scargle periodogram is employed to evaluate the power spectral density for frequencies below 10 Hz. TIP-INN to model Burgers viscoelastic materials Figure S4 shows the predicted viscosity and Young’s modulus for on the testing data considering a Burgers viscoelastic model. Notice that Burgers model can be effectively approximated by the Maxwell model with a nonlinear Young’s modulus (left column). Moreover, the same macroscopic stressstrain response for Burgers model can be achieved through different combinations of internal linear/nonlinear material properties. Thus, the predicted material properties are observed to be significantly different from the true solutions. The cyclic and monotonically oscillatory loads used to train TIP-INN are plotted in Figure S5, along with the predicted stress, Helmholtz free energy and internal variables. The training results lead to conclusions consistent with those obtained from the testing data (see Section 3.2). In the case with two internal variables,

chunks.text: The cyclic and monotonically oscillatory loads used to train TIP-INN are plotted in Figure S5, along with the predicted stress, Helmholtz free energy and internal variables. The training results lead to conclusions consistent with those obtained from the testing data (see Section 3.2). In the case with two internal variables, TIP-INN predicts two nearly identical internal variables. Consequently, the corresponding predicted viscosities (Figure S4) are very similar, as are the associated internal stresses (Figure 3). Gibbs free energy In this work, we consider the sign-flipped Gibbs free energy, that is, G+(σ, α) = −G(σ, α) (see Eq. 13), to model isothermal problems. G+ is non-negative and convex with respect to σ and α. Therefore, the same neural networks used to predict the Helmholtz free energy and dual dissipation in TIPINN can be applied to predict G+ here. Figure S6 shows the detailed architecture for predicting G+ with the loss function defined as LGibbs = Lϵ + LLF + LG+ + + LG+(0) + Lϵ(0) + LL2 , where Lϵ = 1 n=1  ϵθ(n)−ϵ(n) 2 , n=1  θ (n)−G+2 , + = 1 n=1 θ (n) ,  θ (n = 1; t = 0) 2 , Lϵ(0) =  ϵθ(n = 1; t = 0) 2 , Nw Nw n=1  wi 2 . Lϵ and LLF are the losses for the strain and LegendreFenchel transformation, respectively. Other loss functions are defined in the same way as in Eq. 17. Figure S6 shows that accurate G+ are predicted by the convex neural network for the Maxwell viscoelastic model. This numerical example confirms that different types of thermodynamic potentials can be learned using the same computational frameworks that were proposed in this work. Elasto-plastic materials In order to demonstrate the versatility of TIP-INN, we further considered the Prandtl elastoplastic model, which exhibits sharp transitions in the stress-strain relationship. A single internal variable is employed in TIPINN. Figure S7 shows the predicted stress and Helmholtz free energy. While the stress is accurately captured, large errors are observed in the predicted Helmholtz free energy. As the material response evolves from elasticity to plasticity, TIPINN struggles to capture the sharp transition. Increasing the network depth or modifying the activation functions does not significantly improve the accuracy of the predicted Helmholtz free energy. This limitation stems from the inability of the neural integrator to capture the yield surface effectively. Replacing the neural integrator with a Newton-based returnmapping algorithm yields accurate predictions of the yield surface [33], but with a much higher computational cost. Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Potentials (SINP) To overcome the black-box nature of the trained neural potentials, we apply a Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Potentials (SINP) procedure-adapted from the SINP framework [7]-to distill the learned Helmholtz free energy, Ψ, into closedform analytical expressions that can be directly imported into continuum geodynamic codes. Figure S8 shows the SINPcalibrated Ψ against the neural predictions. Notice that an exact quadratic Ψ function (Truth: Ψ = ε2 e = (ε −α1)2) was discovered for the Maxwell model. It is also observed that the convex and positive properties of Ψ were also maintained by the discovered formulas for other models. Fitting the data model

Fitting the data model with non-linear visco-elastic model This section summarizes the details of the Bayesian framework for identifying non-linear viscoelastic constitutive laws, specifically focusing on the evolution of material properties as a function of strain. We are preparing a separate manuscript with additional details of the experiments and a larger sample suite of lava flow samples. Strain Stress Free energy Internal var. Non-Linear Viscoelastic Model Formulation To capture the time-dependent deformation observed in the Pacaya basaltic lava flows [23], we employ a non-linear viscoelastic framework that extends the generalized Maxwell model [75]. Unlike standard subsurface models that assume static material properties, this approach defines the elastic moduli (E) and viscosities (η) as evolving functions of the internal state, represented here by the cumulative axial strain (ϵ). As seen in the experimental mechanical data, the samples exhibit significant non-linearity and creep that require this dynamic parameterization. The total stress σ at any time t is calculated using a recursive direct-fit method [75], which utilizes a history state variable to bypass the computational cost of storing the full strain history and allow fast MCMC inversion. The governing equations for the strain-dependent case are: σ(ϵ, t) = n i=1 Ei(ϵ) dϵi dt dt + n i=1 ( hi[ϵ(t), t]e−∆t/τi + Ei(ϵ)(1 −e−∆t/τi) ∆t/τi dϵi dt dt ) (2) Where the history variable hi represents the viscoelastic state at the previous increment: hi[ϵ(t), t]e−∆t/τi = Z t n Ei(ϵ)e−(t−∆t+τ)/τio dϵi dt dt (3) Evolution of Material Potentials The model accounts for damage-induced degradation, such as pore collapse and microcracking. The

chunks.title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
chunks.text: + Ei(ϵ)(1 −e−∆t/τi) ∆t/τi dϵi dt dt ) (2) Where the history variable hi represents the viscoelastic state at the previous increment: hi[ϵ(t), t]e−∆t/τi = Z t n Ei(ϵ)e−(t−∆t+τ)/τio dϵi dt dt (3) Evolution of Material Potentials The model accounts for damage-induced degradation, such as pore collapse and microcracking. The long-term elastic modulus is held constant as E = a, while each of the viscoelastic moduli Ei (for the elastic element outside the Maxwell element as well as an internal visco-elastic element) and viscosities ηi evolve according to power-law relations based on normalized strain: Ei(ϵ) = a  1 + b  ϵ ϵmax c (4) ηi(ϵ, t) = d 1 + e  ϵ ϵmax f! (5) The relaxation time τi is self-consistently derived from these evolving properties: τi(ηi, Ei, t) = 10ηi(ϵ) Ei(ϵ) (6) Bayesian Parameter Identification (MCMC) To resolve the structural non-uniqueness inherent in rock rheology where multiple parameter combinations can yield similar stress-strain curves and also to prevent robustness against local minima, we utilize a two-stage optimization and inference pipeline. Stage 1: Global Optimization. We maximize a log-likelihood function (log L) that quantifies the residuals between experimental stress σdata and model predictions σmodel: log L(σ) = −1 n i=1 (σdata(ti) −σmodel(ti; θ))2 (7) The parameter space θ is sampled using the Tree-structured Parzen Estimator (TPE) algorithm via the Optuna package [1]. Stage 2: Posterior Estimation. The optimal parameters from Stage 1 initialize a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampler using the emcee package [34]. This yields the corner plots (Fig. 3c, d) used to estimate the full posterior probability density function (PDF), ensuring that parameters are wellconstrained and physically consistent. Comparison to Standard Subsurface Models Typical geodynamic models often rely on linear elastic assumptions that fail to capture the irreversible deformation TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks and creep observed in volcanic basalts [83]. Our results for the Pacaya core and base samples (p5914) demonstrate that elastic moduli can increase by factors as high as 100s as the material compacts or damages [23]. The integration of Bayesian MCMC allows us to quantify the uncertainty in these transitions, identifying the precise moment the material shifts from purely elastic to complex viscoelastic regimes. However, even with the non-linearity, the model has strong challenges with fully representing the stress data. Fig. S9. Experimental stress data was compared to the three different forward models

The red lines correspond to best fit parameter, while blue line refers to the median of all the MCMC parameters. Rate-State Friction in GSM Theory This note provides a rigorous thermodynamic mapping of the empirical Dieterich-Ruina Rate-and-State Friction (RSF) equations [82, 62, 76, 61, 77] into the framework of Generalized Solid Mechanics (GSM). We demonstrate that the empirical RSF laws violate the integrability conditions required for a single dissipation potential, necessitating a bipotential (nonassociated) formulation [26, 25] with two independent pseudopotentials: one for slip velocity and one for state evolution. By decoupling the mechanical and configurational dissipative mechanisms, we derive both the Aging and Slip laws exactly while guaranteeing strict adherence to the Clausius-Duhem inequality. Kinematics and State Variables We consider a representative elementary area of a fault interface. The total displacement jump δ is additively decomposed into an elastic component δe and an inelastic (plastic) slip component δp: δ = δe + δp (8) The thermodynamic state is described by q = (δe, θ), where θ [s] is the internal state variable representing the average contact lifetime of the asperity population. The Helmholtz Free Energy (Ψ) The reversible energy storage is governed by the Helmholtz free energy density: Ψ(δe, θ) = 1 2 k(δe)2 + U(θ) (9) where k is the effective elastic stiffness and U(θ) is the internal surface energy density. Consistent with the logarithmic RSF framework, we adopt: U(θ) = −bσnDc ln  θ θ0  (10) yielding the thermodynamic forces: τ = ∂Ψ ∂δe = kδe (11) Aθ = −∂Ψ ∂θ = bσnDc θ (12) Here τ is the macroscopic

chunks.title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
chunks.text: stiffness and U(θ) is the internal surface energy density. Consistent with the logarithmic RSF framework, we adopt: U(θ) = −bσnDc ln  θ θ0  (10) yielding the thermodynamic forces: τ = ∂Ψ ∂δe = kδe (11) Aθ = −∂Ψ ∂θ = bσnDc θ (12) Here τ is the macroscopic shear stress and Aθ is the state affinity, representing the configurational force driving contact maturation. Note that Aθ > 0 for all admissible θ > 0 and is O(1), scaling with the state evolution parameter b. The Bipotential Framework (Non-Associated Dissipation) The empirical equations cannot be derived from a single dissipation potential Φ∗(τ, Aθ) because they violate the integrability (Maxwell) condition: ∂Aθ ̸= ∂˙θ ∂τ (13) Specifically, the empirical slip law V (τ) has no dependence on Aθ (the left side vanishes), whereas ˙θ depends on τ through V (the right side is non-zero). Therefore, RSF constitutes a non-associated dissipative system requiring two distinct dual dissipation potentials [21, 47]: 1.Slip Potential Φ∗ V (τ; θ): Governs the mechanical slip rate, with θ as a parameter. 2.Healing Potential Φ∗ θ(Aθ; τ, θ): Governs the state evolution kinetics, with the current stress state (or equivalently V ) appearing as a parametric variable. The kinetic equations are given by the decoupled normality rules: V ≡˙δp = ∂Φ∗ ∂τ (14) ˙θ = ∂Φ∗ θ ∂Aθ (15) We define the state-dependent reference yield surface (threshold stress): τth(θ) = σn  f0 + b ln  θ θ0  (16) where σn is the effective normal stress (positive in compression), f0 is the reference friction coefficient, and a, b are the direct and evolution effect parameters, respectively. Derivation of the Dieterich Aging Law To recover the Aging Law exactly, we define the following bipotential pair: Slip Potential: V (τ; θ) = aσnV0 exp  τ −τth(θ) aσn  (17) Healing Potential: θ(Aθ; τ, θ) = Aθ  1 −θV0 Dc exp  τ −τth(θ) aσn  = Aθ  1 −V θ Dc  (18) where V in Eq. 18 is understood as the instantaneous slip velocity determined by the current τ via Eq. 14. Slip Velocity (V ) Applying Eq. 14 to Eq. 17: ∂τ = V0 exp  τ −τth(θ) aσn  (19) Inverting this yields the exact RSF stress equation: τ = σn  f0 + a ln  + b ln  θ θ0  (20) Note that no asymptotic approximation is

chunks.title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
chunks.text: Velocity (V ) Applying Eq. 14 to Eq. 17: ∂τ = V0 exp  τ −τth(θ) aσn  (19) Inverting this yields the exact RSF stress equation: τ = σn  f0 + a ln  + b ln  θ θ0  (20) Note that no asymptotic approximation is required here since the mechanical response is exactly decoupled from the state affinity Aθ by construction. State Evolution Applying Eq. 15 to Eq. 18: ˙θ = ∂Φ∗ θ ∂Aθ = 1 −V θ Dc (21) This is the exact Dieterich Aging Law. The non-associated structure is evident in the equations since the state evolution depends on the mechanical state (V ), but is derived from a potential distinct from that governing the slip itself. Derivation of the Ruina Slip Law The Slip Law requires zero healing when V = 0. We retain the same slip potential Φ∗ V (Eq. 17) but modify the healing potential: Healing Potential (Slip Law): θ(Aθ; τ, θ) = −Aθ  V θ Dc ln  V θ Dc  (22) where V is again the instantaneous velocity from Eq. 19. State Evolution Differentiating Eq. 22: ˙θ = ∂Φ∗ θ ∂Aθ = −V θ Dc ln  V θ Dc  (23) This is the exact Ruina Slip Law. When V →0, the term V ln(V ) →0, enforcing ˙θ = 0 as required. Thermodynamic Admissibility and High-Velocity Breakdown The foundational requirement of any physically sound constitutive model is adherence to the Second Law of Thermodynamics. In the context of isothermal Generalized Solid Mechanics, the intrinsic dissipation D must satisfy the Clausius-Duhem inequality for all physically admissible states: D = τV + Aθ ˙θ ≥0 (24) Thermodynamic Breakdown of the Slip Law Evaluating the Ruina Slip Law exposes a critical theoretical limitation. Substituting the exact state evolution (Eq. 23) into the dissipation inequality yields: DSlip = τV −bσnDc θ  V θ Dc ln  V θ Dc   τ −bσn ln  V θ Dc  (25) We explicitly expand the logarithmic term and substitute the exact macroscopic stress τ (Eq. 20): DSlip = V σn   f0 + a ln  + b ln  θ θ0  −b ln(V ) −b ln(θ) + b ln(Dc)  (26) The state variable θ cancels identically from the dissipation equation (+b ln(θ) −b ln(θ) = 0). Expanding the reference velocity and grouping the remaining

chunks.title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
chunks.text: V σn   f0 + a ln  + b ln  θ θ0  −b ln(V ) −b ln(θ) + b ln(Dc)  (26) The state variable θ cancels identically from the dissipation equation (+b ln(θ) −b ln(θ) = 0). Expanding the reference velocity and grouping the remaining terms results in an exact, purely velocity-dependent expression for the intrinsic dissipation: DSlip = V σn h f0 + (a −b) ln(V ) −a ln(V0) −b ln(θ0) + b ln(Dc) i (27) This exact thermodynamic reduction reveals a fundamental physical violation at extreme slip rates. In the seismogenic regime, faults are characteristically velocity-weakening, necessitating that b > a. Consequently, the coefficient (a −b) is strictly negative. As the slip velocity approaches high velocity speeds (V → ∞), the term (a −b) ln(V ) →−∞. Since all other terms within the bracket are bounded constants, the total intrinsic dissipation inevitably becomes negative at sufficiently high velocities (though this may not be physically relevant for real faults) : lim V →∞DSlip →−∞ for b > a (28) This proves that the unregularized empirical Ruina Slip Law is not globally thermodynamically admissible. While the bipotential GSM framework flawlessly maps the algebraic structure of the empirical equations, it formally dictates that the logarithmic RSF formulation cannot govern dynamic rupture. To prevent violations of the Second Law of Thermodynamics, computational models must regularize the slip potential at high velocities (e.g., via sinh-based reaction rate theory) to ensure bounded, positive dissipation. TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks Admissibility of the Aging Law For the Dieterich Aging Law, substituting the state affinity (Eq. 12) and the exact state evolution (Eq. 21) yields: DAging = τV + bσnDc θ  1 −V θ Dc  = V (τ −bσn) + bσnDc θ (29) Substituting the macroscopic stress τ (Eq. 20) and grouping terms gives: DAging = V σn  f0 −b + a ln  + b ln  θ θ0  + bσnDc θ (30) The term bσnDc/θ represents the rate of free energy dissipation associated with autonomous contact aging and is strictly positive for θ > 0. During slip-weakening ( ˙θ < 0), the stored surface energy of the contacts is released, contributing negatively to the local dissipation budget, but the positive mechanical work (τV ) globally dominates. The Aging Law thus remains thermodynamically admissible across standard operational regimes, though

chunks.title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
chunks.text: is strictly positive for θ > 0. During slip-weakening ( ˙θ < 0), the stored surface energy of the contacts is released, contributing negatively to the local dissipation budget, but the positive mechanical work (τV ) globally dominates. The Aging Law thus remains thermodynamically admissible across standard operational regimes, though similar to the Slip law above, the aging law has the identical high-V breakdown. Regularization for Global Thermodynamic Admissibility The previous section demonstrated that the classical logarithmic Slip Law inevitably violates the Clausius-Duhem inequality at dynamic rupture velocities (V →∞). To rectify this, the bipotential framework must be regularized at both asymptotic limits. We apply absolute reaction-rate theory to resolve the singularity at V →0, and demonstrate that elastodynamic (radiation) damping is required to enforce thermodynamic consistency at V →∞. Reaction-Rate Regularization (The sinh Law) The empirical logarithmic slip equation implies a unidirectional crossing of asperity energy barriers, resulting in an anti-physical singularity at V = 0. Following transition-state theory [30, 41] and work in numerical fault modeling [55, 77], the slip potential must account for both forward and backward microscopic jumps. We construct a regularized, globally convex slip potential using the hyperbolic cosine: V (τ; θ) = 2aσnV0  cosh  τ −τth(θ) aσn  −1  (31) The subtraction of unity ensures that Φ∗ V (τth, θ) = 0 and Φ∗ 0 for all admissible states. Applying the normality rule (Eq. 14) yields the regularized slip equation: ∂τ = 2V0 sinh  τ −τth(θ) aσn  (32) Inverting this provides the exact, regularized macroscopic shear stress: τ = τth(θ) + aσn arcsinh  = σn  f0 + a arcsinh  + b ln  θ θ0  (33) This regularization resolves the zero-velocity singularity, as arcsinh(0) = 0, allowing the interface to arrest without generating infinite negative stress. The Persistence of the High-Velocity Violation While the sinh potential grounds the near-zero velocity regime physically, we must evaluate its high-velocity thermodynamic behavior. Substituting the regularized stress (Eq. 33) into the dissipation expression for the Slip Law yields: DReg = V σn  f0 + a arcsinh  + b ln  θ θ0  −b ln  V θ Dc  (34) Once again, the state variable θ cancels identically. Regrouping terms gives: DReg = V σn  f0 + a arcsinh  −b ln(V ) −b ln(θ0) + b ln(Dc)  (35) To evaluate the dynamic rupture

chunks.text: b ln  θ θ0  −b ln  V θ Dc  (34) Once again, the state variable θ cancels identically. Regrouping terms gives: DReg = V σn  f0 + a arcsinh  −b ln(V ) −b ln(θ0) + b ln(Dc)  (35) To evaluate the dynamic rupture limit, we employ the asymptotic expansion of the inverse hyperbolic sine as V →∞: arcsinh  = ln  + s V 2 + 1   −−−−→ln  (36) Substituting this limit into the dissipation expression yields: lim V →∞DReg ≈V σn [(a −b) ln(V ) −a ln(V0) −b ln(θ0) + b ln(Dc)] (37) This is a critical theoretical result: the sinh regularization fails to prevent the Second Law violation at high velocities. Because the physical transition-state barrier is entirely overcome at extreme shear, the sinh function reverts to logarithmic scaling. In the velocity-weakening regime (b > a), the (a −b) ln(V ) term still drives the total intrinsic dissipation to negative infinity: lim V →∞DReg →−∞ for b > a (38) Thermodynamic Resolution via Viscous Damping The mathematical failure of both logarithmic and sinh potentials at high slip rates implies that frictional constitutive laws alone cannot describe dynamic rupture. parallel dissipative mechanism is required to bound the energy budget. Physically, at seismic slip rates (∼ m/s), the fault interface radiates elastodynamic energy (radiation damping) and experiences phonon drag [76]. In the GSM framework, this is incorporated by adding a linear viscous stress ηV to the macroscopic stress, where η ≈G/2cs (shear modulus G, shear wave speed cs). The fully regularized macroscopic stress becomes: τtotal = σn  f0 + a arcsinh  + b ln  θ θ0  | {z } τRSF +ηV (39) The corresponding total intrinsic dissipation incorporates the viscous penalty: DT otal = τRSF V + Aθ ˙θ | {z } DReg +ηV 2 (40) At the dynamic rupture limit (V →∞), the dominant terms are: DT otal ≈ηV 2 −σn|a −b|V ln(V ) + O(V ) (41) Because the polynomial viscous dissipation (ηV 2) grows strictly faster than the logarithmic deficit (V ln V ), the quadratic term unconditionally dominates. Therefore: lim V →∞DT otal →+∞≥0 (42) This proves that while the sinh potential ensures physical admissibility at V →0, the inclusion of high-velocity viscous (radiation) damping is strictly required to ensure global thermodynamic admissibility of the Rate-and-State framework across all slip regimes.

chunks.title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
chunks.text: Article https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-026-70033-x Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava ﬂow interaction at Axial volcano Han Wu 1,2, Wenxin Xie1, Satish C. Singh , Hélène Carton 1, Graham M. Kent1,3, Adrien F. Arnulf 4 & Alistair J. Harding5 Magmatically-accreted upper crust along mid-ocean ridges is traditionally considered to consist of lava ﬂows overlying a sheeted-dyke complex. However, how the upper crust is formed at hotspot-inﬂuenced ridge segments remains unknown. Using three-dimensional seismic reﬂection data, here we report images of lava ﬂow layering beneath Axial volcano on the Juan de Fuca Ridge. These lava ﬂow layers dip inward towards the caldera and rift zones, resulting from subsidence caused by magma withdrawal beneath the summit and rift zone extension. Furthermore, our images reveal that the classic, laterally continuous sheeted-dyke complex is absent. Instead, lava ﬂow layers are rotated and brought into direct contact with the magma domain, leading to partial assimilation of these units. Three-dimensional images also show that melt is injected outward along lava ﬂow layers, suggesting that the upper crust at Axial volcano is formed by complex interactions between injected melt sills and lava ﬂow layers. The oceanic crust, which comprises two-thirds of the Earth’s surface, is formed through partial melting of the mantle, where upwelling melt migrates towards the surface and accumulates in crustal magma reservoirs1–3. Some of this melt is erupted through feeder dykes, with erupted lava ﬂows at the surface and intruded dykes forming the upper oceanic crust (Layer 2), while the residual melt cools through hydrothermal circulation and crystallises, forming the lower gabbroic crust (Layer 3)4. This architecture is the classic layer-cake oceanic crustal structure as described in the Penrose model5. Using ophiolite6 and drilling studies as a framework7,8, seismic investigations of the upper crust9–12 have further divided it into Layer 2A and Layer 2B, where Layer 2A is interpreted to consist of lava ﬂows and Layer 2B of sheeted dykes, although hydrothermal alteration has also been invoked as a possible mechanism explaining the location of the seismically-deﬁned Layer 2A/2B boundary13. Although lava ﬂows and sheeted dyke sequences can be unambiguously distinguished within ophiolites, drill cores, and tectonic windows14,15, their in-situ characterisations using seismic methods have been difﬁcult. This difﬁculty is because lava ﬂow stratigraphy has not been seismically imaged thus far, and vertical or steeply dipping dykes are inherently difﬁcult to image using either reﬂected or refracted waves. Furthermore, the boundary between lava ﬂow

chunks.title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
chunks.text: cores, and tectonic windows14,15, their in-situ characterisations using seismic methods have been difﬁcult. This difﬁculty is because lava ﬂow stratigraphy has not been seismically imaged thus far, and vertical or steeply dipping dykes are inherently difﬁcult to image using either reﬂected or refracted waves. Furthermore, the boundary between lava ﬂow and dyke sequences is not sharp, instead there is a high velocity gradient zone separating low-velocity lava ﬂow layers above with the high-velocity dyke sequence below4. Therefore, the main methods to locate the Layer 2A/2B boundary has been the stacking of wide-angle energy in multi-channel seismic (MCS) data produced by the high velocity gradient at Layer 2A/B boundary11 (Fig. 1a) or its identiﬁcation is based on velocity contours and velocity gradients obtained using tomographic methods16 (Fig. 1b). In order to image lava ﬂow stratigraphy, threedimensional (3D) pre-stack depth migration (PSDM) methodology is required to adequately refocus the seismic energy in the shallow crust (e.g., Fig. 1c), which we have carried out using 3D MCS data from Axial volcano17. Axial volcano lies at the intersection of the intermediatespreading Juan de Fuca Ridge and Cobb hotspot (Fig. 2) and seems to have been formed in the last ~0.5 Ma18–20. It hosts several hydrothermal ﬁelds21,22 and has been the site of three documented eruptions Received: 9 July 2025 Accepted: 13 February 2026 Check for updates 1Université Paris Cité, Institut de Physique du Globe de Paris, CNRS, Paris, France. 2School of Earth Sciences and Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University, Zhuhai, China. 3Nevada Seismological Laboratory, MS-0174, University of Nevada, Reno, NV, USA. 4Amazon, San Diego, CA, USA. 5Cecil H. and Ida M. Green Institute of Geophysics and Planetary Physics, Scripps Institution of Oceanography, University of California San Diego, La Jolla, CA, USA. e-mail: singh@ipgp.fr Nature Communications| (2026) 17:3512 1234567890():,; 1234567890():,; (1998, 2011, 2015)23–26 with the next one considered imminent (as of late 2025) on the basis of geodetic-derived deformation and seismicity rates27. Axial volcano lies at a water depth of about 1.4 km; it displays a nearly ﬂat, 20–40 km wide summit hosting a horseshoe-shaped 8 km × 3 km caldera that separates two rift zones: the north rift zone (NRZ) and south rift zone (SRZ) (Fig. 2)18,28. The current caldera is interpreted to have been formed 1259 +/−119 years BP, based on the ages of large volume lava ﬂows on the distal SRZ29. The volcano is continuously monitored using a sea-to-land, ﬁber-optic cabled observatory, recording

chunks.title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
chunks.text: the north rift zone (NRZ) and south rift zone (SRZ) (Fig. 2)18,28. The current caldera is interpreted to have been formed 1259 +/−119 years BP, based on the ages of large volume lava ﬂows on the distal SRZ29. The volcano is continuously monitored using a sea-to-land, ﬁber-optic cabled observatory, recording earthquake, geodetic, heat ﬂow and chemical data30. In 1998, sparse wide-angle ocean-bottom seismic refraction data were acquired that showed the presence of a large magma body embedded within an up to 11-km-thick oceanic crust31. A 2D MCS dataset collected shortly thereafter in 2002 revealed complex magma sill reﬂections and a Layer 2A/2B boundary16,32,33, similar to images obtained along non-hotspot-inﬂuenced mid-ocean ridges (e.g., Fig. 1a, b)9–12. Based on an updated tomographic velocity model that combined prior on-bottom wide-angle31 and redatumed, 6-km-long 2D streamer data16, the Layer 2 A thickness was found to vary between 0.4 km and 1.4 km, with the thinnest Layer 2 A (0.4–0.7 km) observed just outside of the caldera with a slightly thicker Layer 2A (0.75–0.95 km) centered beneath the caldera32. However, as tomographic images only provide low-resolution information, it is not possible to decipher the ﬁne-scale lava ﬂow layering of this uppermost oceanic crust and investigate relationships to the underlying magmatic system. In 2019, an exceptional 3D MCS dataset was acquired at Axial volcano (Fig. 2; Supplementary Fig. 1), showing the presence of four funnel-shaped melt boundaries beneath the volcano17, interpreted as the top of a magma domain17, within whichmelt sills are distributed in a matrix of possibly crystal mush, and representing the crustal lithosphere-asthenosphere boundary (LAB). Here, we processed the same MCS dataset differently using 3D Kirchhoff PSDM (see Methods), instead of post-stack time migration17. Our processed 3D seismic volume provides the clear images of lava ﬂow stratigraphy within the upper crust and its relationship to the complex underlying magma domain, suggesting that the upper oceanic crust is formed by a complex interaction between melt sills and the extrusive layer within a ridge-hotspot environment. Results and discussion Lava ﬂow layering The 3D PSDM reveals lava ﬂow layering between the seaﬂoor and the magma domain (Supplementary Movies 1 and 2). A block diagram (Fig. 3, Supplementary Fig. 2 and Movie 3) shows lava ﬂow layering beneath the northern (Inline 341) and southern (Inline 178) rift zones, and along a perpendicular proﬁle (Xline 2601) through the caldera. We identify the lava ﬂow layering based on its reﬂection

chunks.title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
chunks.text: Movies 1 and 2). A block diagram (Fig. 3, Supplementary Fig. 2 and Movie 3) shows lava ﬂow layering beneath the northern (Inline 341) and southern (Inline 178) rift zones, and along a perpendicular proﬁle (Xline 2601) through the caldera. We identify the lava ﬂow layering based on its reﬂection characteristics and seismic attributes (Supplementary Fig. 3, see Methods). The top of the highly reﬂective zone, interpreted as a volume containing melt sills, deﬁnes the top of the magma domain, representing the lithosphere-asthenosphere boundary (LAB) (Figs. 2–4)17. Although the term LAB is generally used elsewhere to deﬁne the base of the lithosphere in the upper mantle, here a crustal melt sill beneath the ridge axis separates solid brittle crust above with partially molten lower crust and upper mantle below, and therefore the top of the magma domain at Axial volcano is termed as the LAB17. North of the caldera, lava ﬂow layers from the southeast dip toward the NRZ before becoming ﬂat beneath the NRZ (Fig. 3). Similarly, south of the caldera, lava ﬂow layers dip gently inwards toward the rift zone, indicating a symmetric pattern. The perpendicular Xline proﬁle shows that the layers continue beneath the caldera, but in some cases are offset by small-throw steeply dipping faults with vertical offsets of tens of metres. Four Xlines spaced 5 km apart across the caldera exhibit a similar pattern of lava ﬂow layering in the upper crust, with a pervasive inward-dipping geometry towards the magma domain beneath the caldera (Fig. 4, Supplementary Fig. 4 and Movie 4). As the dip of the layers increases with depth (Fig. 5), some layers have deepened up to 1 km through rotation, with the overall stack of lava ﬂow layers reaching >3 km in thickness. The pattern of inward-dipping lava ﬂow layers towards the magma domain extends southeast of the caldera as seen along Xline proﬁle 2424 (Fig. 4). The dip of these layers varies, depending upon the location within the 3D box, ranging from nearly ﬂat just below the seaﬂoor to dips of up to 18° near the magma domain (Fig. 5). Sometimes, these layers ﬂatten as they approach the magma domain and beneath the rift zones. Rift zone-coincident proﬁles were extracted from the 3D PSDM volume (Fig. 6, Supplementary Fig. 5), which highlight the relationship between lava ﬂow layering, faults, and the underlying magma domain. Although the images along the NRZ are

chunks.title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
chunks.text: these layers ﬂatten as they approach the magma domain and beneath the rift zones. Rift zone-coincident proﬁles were extracted from the 3D PSDM volume (Fig. 6, Supplementary Fig. 5), which highlight the relationship between lava ﬂow layering, faults, and the underlying magma domain. Although the images along the NRZ are relatively poor, layered structures are present. In contrast, layering is very prominent along the SRZ proﬁle down to 3 km below the seaﬂoor (bsf). The analysis of the 3D PSDM volume indicates that the upper crustal layered stratigraphy is present over more than 85% of the study area; only the northeast corner of the 3D box does not exhibit strong layering. Given the frequency bandwidth of our seismic images (5–30 Hz), individual lava ﬂows cannot be imaged, but rather the visible layering likely represents packages of ﬂows, with vertical resolution dependent on wavelength. The vertical resolution is generally a quarter of the wavelength, which for an upper crustal velocity of 3–5 km/s would be ~25–250 m (Supplementary Fig. 3). Wideaperture (6 km source-receiver offset) 3D imaging allows us to image structure on similar lateral scales (250 m). Although the nature of the impedance contrast that produces these reﬂections is unknown, the changes in rock properties of an individual ﬂow, the residence time at 16.3 km Stacking Tomography+FWI Melt Lenses Layer 2A/2B boundary Melt Lenses Melt Lenses Lava flow layering a b Layer 2A/2B boundary Fig. 1 | Different representation of seismic images. a Seismic image obtained using the conventional stacking method showing Layer 2A and melt lens reﬂections (e.g., Harding et al.11) along a 2D proﬁle within the study area. The location of the proﬁle is shown in Supplementary Fig. 1. b The P-wave velocity obtained using a combination of travel time tomography and full waveform inversion (FWI) of same data set16. The blue curve marks a change in the velocity gradient, interpreted as the base of Layer 2A. c 3D pre-stack depth migrated (PSDM) image along a coincident proﬁle (Xline 2356) showing lava ﬂow layering (light yellow) and melt lenses (red) (see below). Note the absence of a clear Layer 2A/2B boundary in the PSDM image. The map shows the north rift zone (NRZ), south rift zone (SRZ), hydrothermal vents (magenta-ﬁlled triangles)21,22 and the lava ﬂows from the last three eruptions in 1998 (blue), 2011 (cyan) and 2015 (green)23–26. The horseshoe-shaped caldera, 8 km × 3 km, is also highlighted. The transparent grey-shaded regions indicate the funnel-shaped upper boundary of the magma domain, interpreted as the lithosphere-asthenosphere boundary (LAB), surfaces extracted from a 3D time migration image17. The thick dashed rectangle indicates the area of the 3D seismic reﬂection box and thin dashed lines mark the inline and crossline numbers. The Inline numbers range from 101 to 538 spaced at 37.5 m and the cross lines (Xlines) range from 1001 to 4200 spaced at 12.5 m, giving rise to a 16.3 km × 40 km 3D box. Article https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-026-70033-x Nature Communications| (2026) 17:3512 the seaﬂoor where seawater alteration occurred, and/or porosity contrasts, likely yields this contrast. Magma domain and melt sill injection Using images from the 3D post-stack time-migrated volume, Kent et al.17 observed four large-scale funnel-shaped boundaries that deﬁne the top of a magma domain. The 3D PSDM imagery presented here conﬁrms those features but also reveals other smaller melt sills of various sizes outside of the identiﬁed magma domain17, which suggests that the inﬂuence of the magma domain may extend further east of the caldera (Fig. 7, Supplementary Fig. 6 and Movie 5). For example, within proﬁle Inline 373 shown in the bottom left of Fig. 7, there are six sill-like melt bodies imaged; but only two of the most southeasterly melt sills were previously reported17. A critical observation is that the lava ﬂow layers not only abut the sides of the magma domain but, in some locations, melt appears to be injected outwards between lava ﬂow layers above the magma domain (Fig. 8, Supplementary Fig. 7). However, it is sometimes difﬁcult to distinguish between the lava ﬂow layers, injected melt sills between lava ﬂow layers, and small melt sills that form the top of the magma domain. Here, we distinguish intruded melt sills from lava ﬂow layers based on their

However, it is sometimes difﬁcult to distinguish between the lava ﬂow layers, injected melt sills between lava ﬂow layers, and small melt sills that form the top of the magma domain. Here, we distinguish intruded melt sills from lava ﬂow layers based on their strong reﬂectivity, large instantaneous amplitude, instantaneous phase with positive to negative transition, lower frequency content, complex shapes, stratigraphic relationships, and depth (Supplementary Fig. 3). Article https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-026-70033-x Nature Communications| (2026) 17:3512 domain downdip, this event is interpreted as an upward injected melt sill (Fig. 7, Supplementary Fig. 6). Moreover, as these melt sills cool within the lava ﬂow layers, the interface would still produce a strong impedance contrast that enables imaging a distinct reﬂection as opposed to the typical lava ﬂow layers observed closer to the seaﬂoor (Fig. 6, Supplementary Fig. 5). Lava ﬂow layering within the caldera above the magma domain is more chaotic than outside the caldera, apparently disrupted by magmatic and hydrothermal processes (dyking, sill injection) or by faulting and block rotation during caldera collapse events (Fig. 8, Inline 241). Lava ﬂow layering, faults and rotated layers are clearly visible on seismic images (Fig. 7, Inline 241). Some of

chunks.title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
chunks.text: is more chaotic than outside the caldera, apparently disrupted by magmatic and hydrothermal processes (dyking, sill injection) or by faulting and block rotation during caldera collapse events (Fig. 8, Inline 241). Lava ﬂow layering, faults and rotated layers are clearly visible on seismic images (Fig. 7, Inline 241). Some of the shallow faults above the magma domain are distinctly imaged, with curved geometries and rotated layers (Fig. 8, inset). Away from the caldera, one can clearly see interaction between lava ﬂow layers and melt sill intrusions (Fig. 7, Inline 373; Fig. 8, Inline 424). On the other hand, vertical dykes and hydrothermal conduits are inherently difﬁcult to image. Subsidence Mapping of lava ﬂows from the past three eruptions23–26 shows that these events initiated from dyke intrusion along the eastern edge of the caldera34, possibly sourced from the shallowest part of the magma domain17. From that nucleation point, the dykes propagated either north or south along the rift zones for distances up to 20–30 km from the caldera with lava outpouring from ﬁssures along the rifts24–26. Within the caldera, recent sheet ﬂows have thicknesses of 3–5 m near eruptive features increasing to ~15 m down the slope25,26,35,36. Awayfrom the caldera, lava ﬂows erupted along the rift zones form pillow mounds and ridges with thickness reaching >120 m24,26. Even though the seaﬂoor smoothly dips away from the caldera, the seismically imaged lava ﬂow layers show a gentle dip either towards the magma domain, rift zones, and/or the caldera. The lava ﬂow piles thicken as they approach the rift zones and the underlying magma domain, with the largest subsidence evident along the rift zones and within the caldera. This geometry suggests that the lava ﬂow layers have subsided after their formation. There are two likely mechanisms that can cause the observed localised subsidence: (1) extension along rift zones, and (2) emptying of magma reservoirs during occasional large-volume eruptions that cause caldera collapse. As the lava ﬂow layering in the seismic imagery generally thickens toward the caldera and rift zones, it is likely that this pattern is simply the result of inﬁlling of space caused by the subsidence. However, the terminations of the deepest imaged lava ﬂow layer are not always located beneath the rift zones; instead, some are shifted either towards the caldera or the large melt funnels17. The horizontal wavelength of subsidence is on the scale of 10–15 km, and such long-wavelength

chunks.title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
chunks.text: caused by the subsidence. However, the terminations of the deepest imaged lava ﬂow layer are not always located beneath the rift zones; instead, some are shifted either towards the caldera or the large melt funnels17. The horizontal wavelength of subsidence is on the scale of 10–15 km, and such long-wavelength subsidence is likely due to the cumulative effects of major withdrawal events from the sub-caldera magma domain (Fig. 9). Ridge-ward dipping lava ﬂow layers have also been observed in Iceland, both east and west of the ridge axis37,38. There too, the dip of lava ﬂow layers increases with depth from nearly horizontal at shallow depth to 5–10° over 10–15 km distance. Drilling results indicate that lava ﬂow layers that lie at 1.5–2.0 km depth were deposited on land or in shallow water environment39,40. Gradual sagging of lava ﬂow layers due to continuous loading of lava ﬂows in the rift zone was invoked to explain deepening of lava ﬂows towards the rift zone for Iceland37,41, but here we show that emptying of magma reservoir during large volcanic eruption might be the main process for the increasing dips towards the rift zones and caldera. Seaward dipping reﬂections (SDRs) commonly observed along volcanic continental margins42,43 resemble the dipping lava ﬂow reﬂections observed at Axial volcano. Taken together, the lava ﬂow layering in Iceland and at Axial volcano may represent a special class of crustal accretion at ocean spreading centres occurring along magmatically enhanced sections of mid-ocean ridges, such as those inﬂuenced by ridge-plume interaction41,44,45. Assimilation On the basis of seismic reﬂection images of the funnel-shaped upper surface of the magma domain observed down to 6 km depth, Kent et al.17 hypothesised that assimilation of dykes and gabbroic rocks has been ongoing, as a consequence of a phase of enhanced magma supply currently taking place at Axial volcano. In addition to evidence of melt sill injection along lava ﬂow layers, our images show that some of these inward-dipping lava ﬂow layers are in direct contact with the magma Fig. 8 | Melt sills injected along lava ﬂow layers. The location of inset (bottom left corner) is shown by black box in upper right of Inline 241. Inline 424 and Xline 2424 highlight melt sills injected along lava

Lava ﬂowlayers are highlightedin yellow, magma domain sills in red, injected sills in orange, and faults in magenta. Injected melt sills have different orientations with some dipping southwest back toward the caldera (right edge of Xline 2424), while others dip to the northwest (right edge of Inline 424). Article https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-026-70033-x Nature Communications| (2026) 17:3512 domain boundary (Figs. 4, 7 and 8). If these lava ﬂow layers were initially emplaced at the seaﬂoor, they would have had higher porosities46,47 ﬁlled with water39,40. As they get buried, heated, and altered40, they eventually lie in contact with the top of the magma domain. The melting temperature of hydrated lava ﬂows is ~800 °C, much less than that of primitive basalt (>1100 °C)48, therefore the contact zone between hydrated lava ﬂow layers and melt within the magma domain could constitute a zone of interaction with assimilation of the lava ﬂow layers directly into the magma domain48 (Fig. 9). Although some primitive lavas erupted during the 2015 eruption, most of the lava emplaced during the three historical eruptions is somewhat chemically evolved36. At this time, there is no direct geochemical evidence of assimilation in the basalt chemistry of erupted lava ﬂows at Axial volcano, the presence of a young andesite ridge along the NRZ north of Axial volcano49 suggests potential remelting and assimilation and/or a high degree of fractionation49, possibly supporting the assimilation hypothesis. Elsewhere, the assimilation of some of the sheeted dyke sequence into the underlying magma chamber has been documented from ophiolites and laboratory experiments48,50, but the possible assimilation of basaltic lava ﬂows has not been considered until now. Iceland is an archetypical example for the observation of remelting and assimilation of crustal rocks, with at least 10% of lavas of rhyolitic origin51,52. Rhyolitic melt was unexpectedly encountered at 2.1 km

chunks.title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
chunks.text: ophiolites and laboratory experiments48,50, but the possible assimilation of basaltic lava ﬂows has not been considered until now. Iceland is an archetypical example for the observation of remelting and assimilation of crustal rocks, with at least 10% of lavas of rhyolitic origin51,52. Rhyolitic melt was unexpectedly encountered at 2.1 km depth during the Kraﬂa Testbed Drilling project in 200953,54. Although several hypotheses have been proposed for the origin of these rhyolites, melting and assimilation of wall rock51 or partial melting of hydrated and hydrothermally altered crust53 remain the main hypotheses. Assimilation has also been reported in the Oman ophiolite4,48, at a slow spreading ridge55 and at an ultra-slow spreading ridge56 and hence assimilation might be more widespread along the mid-ocean ridge system than previously realised, especially at sites with enhanced magma supply, such as those located at a ridge-plume intersections. Hydrated-dehydrated boundary So where is the sheeted-dyke complex? Although some vertical dykes must be present above the magma domain and along the rift zones, the imaging of thick lava ﬂow layers, some of which are in direct contact with the magma domain, suggests that the classic thick sheeted dyke sequence of Layer 2B is absent beneath Axial volcano, and the crust predominantly consists of lava ﬂows and gabbro. This observation is at odds with previous studies of the upper crust at Axial volcano16,32, where the upper crust was inferred to consist of two layers: lava ﬂows and sheeted dyke complex. Instead, the 3D seismic imagery presented here suggests that the upper crust is mostly formed by lava ﬂows and melt-sill interaction. It is certain that dykes feed magma to the surface during eruptions, but a combination of dyke assimilation and lateral sill intrusions within the lava ﬂow layers may erase the evidence of the dyking process. However, since dykes are the main conduits of erupted lavas, extending for tens of kilometres along the rift zones, a dyke sequence might be present along the rift zones away from the summit of Axial volcano and along the non-hotspot-inﬂuenced ridge segments of the Juan du Fuca Ridge. Based on a comprehensive tomographic study16, it was suggested that the maximum thickness of Layer 2A (i.e., presumed lava ﬂows) was ~1.4 km. However, 3D seismic reﬂection images show lava ﬂow layering down to >3 km depth bsf, suggesting that the conventional Layer 2A/ 2B boundary does not represent a lava ﬂow–sheeted dyke boundary beneath Axial

chunks.title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
chunks.text: it was suggested that the maximum thickness of Layer 2A (i.e., presumed lava ﬂows) was ~1.4 km. However, 3D seismic reﬂection images show lava ﬂow layering down to >3 km depth bsf, suggesting that the conventional Layer 2A/ 2B boundary does not represent a lava ﬂow–sheeted dyke boundary beneath Axial volcano. Instead, it may correspond to the depth of melt sill injection into the lava ﬂows. We envision that when hot molten rock (melt sills) is injected into the upper crustal lava ﬂow layers, the heat would metamorphose and dehydrate the nearby lava ﬂow layers. This would make the rock denser and increase the contrast in seismic velocities. Over time, as more melt sills are injected and solidify, the seismic velocity differences between the altered lava ﬂow layers and the solidiﬁed melt sills would gradually diminish. The combined effect would progressively increase the seismic velocities of deeper layers, which could explain the velocity boundary that creates the classical Layer 2A/2B seismic reﬂections observed at Axial volcano (Figs. 1b and 10). Such intrusion of melt sills into lava ﬂow layers has also been observed in the lower sequences of Icelandic lava ﬂows57, and in SDRs along volcanic continental margins58. Subsequent metamorphism (in the greenschist and amphibolite facies) leads to a signiﬁcant increase in the P-wave velocity at this contact surface58. To further investigate the transition between shallow lava ﬂows and their deeper intruded counterparts, seismic full waveform inversion (FWI) was performed along one of the long-offset 2D proﬁles (having a maximum offset of 12 km, see Methods), to determine the high-resolution P-wave velocity structure of the upper crust (Fig. 10). By combining conventional imaging and FWI results, we ﬁnd that the conventional base of Layer 2A from stacking of wide-angle reﬂection data corresponds to a high Fig. 9 | A model of crustal accretion at plume-inﬂuenced ridges. Schematic diagram showing the crustal accretion by injected melt sill and lava ﬂow interaction during a strong magmatic phase (right). Yellow curves indicate subsiding lava ﬂow layers injected by melt sills (in orange). Assimilation occurs at the lithosphereasthenosphere boundary (LAB, in red). Blue dashed line indicates the possible location of the classical Layer 2A/2B boundaryduring the magmatic phase. Note the absence of a sheeted dyke sequence. In contrast, when magmatism wanes (left), the LAB becomes an accreting boundary as magmas become less evolved. Blue and light blue lines indicate gabbroic accretion. Article https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-026-70033-x Nature
