chunks.title: Anisotropic tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone
chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors 318 (2021) 106767 Available online 3 July 2021 0031-9201/© The Authors. Published by Elsevier This is an open access article under the license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/). Anisotropic tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone Dapeng Zhao a,*, Yuanyuan Hua a,b,* a Department of Geophysics, Graduate School of Science, Tohoku University, Sendai 980-8578, Japan b Guangzhou Institute of Geochemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, CAS Center for Excellence in Deep Earth Science, Guangzhou 510640, China A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: Cascadia Subduction zone Seismic tomography Seismic anisotropy Azimuthal anisotropy Radial anisotropy Earthquake Volcano A B S T R A C T The first P-wave tomography of 3-D azimuthal and radial anisotropy of the Cascadia subduction zone is deter­ mined by inverting local and teleseismic arrival-time data. Fast-velocity directions (FVDs) of azimuthal anisot­ ropy in the crust are generally trench-parallel, reflecting N-S compression along the Cascadia margin. Radial anisotropy (RAN) is negative (i.e., Vpv > Vph) in the crust and upper-mantle wedge beneath the Cascadia volcanoes and back-arc area, reflecting hot and wet upwelling flows associated with fluids from dehydration reactions of the young and warm Juan de Fuca plate that is subducting toward the northeast. Trench-parallel FVDs occur in the subducting slab under the forearc, suggesting that the gently-dipping slab may still keep its original anisotropy produced at the mid-ocean ridge and modified at the outer-rise before subduction. The slab and subslab mantle exhibit the same RAN pattern: positive RAN in the Cascadia forearc whereas negative RAN under the Cascadia volcanoes and the back-arc. This feature suggests that the slab and the subslab asthenosphere are strongly coupled, and subslab mantle flow is formed by entrainment of the asthenosphere with the overriding slab. In northern Cascadia, NE-SW FVDs occur in a prominent subslab low-velocity zone that also exhibits negative RAN, reflecting thermally buoyant mantle materials derived from nearby oceanic hotspots, which flow toward the northeast and gradually accumulate under northern Cascadia, resulting in decompression melting. 1. Introduction Subduction zones are convergent plate boundaries characterized geomorphologically by deep ocean trenches and island arcs or conti­ nental margins, seismically by the strongest earthquakes on Earth and a landward dipping plane of intermediate-depth and deep-focus earth­ quakes, tectonically by regional-scale crustal faulting and terrane movements, and volcanically by a linear belt of eruptive centers, the so- called volcanic front (e.g., Zhao, 2015). Subduction zones are

chunks.title: Anisotropic tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone
chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: or conti­ nental margins, seismically by the strongest earthquakes on Earth and a landward dipping plane of intermediate-depth and deep-focus earth­ quakes, tectonically by regional-scale crustal faulting and terrane movements, and volcanically by a linear belt of eruptive centers, the so- called volcanic front (e.g., Zhao, 2015). Subduction zones are one of the key elements in plate tectonics and geodynamics, because subduction and arc magmatism are fundamental processes in the evolution of the Earth. To date, a great number of workers have investigated subduction zones using various approaches including seismic anisotropy and nu­ merical modeling, leading to two simplified endmember models of mantle flow in subduction zones (e.g., Long, 2016). In one model, two- dimensional (2-D) corner flow in the mantle wedge and 2-D entrained flow below the slab are induced by viscous coupling between the sub­ ducting slab and the ambient mantle (Supplementary Fig. S1a). In another model, a three-dimensional (3-D) flow field is induced by trench migration and slab rollback, and the flow is deflected horizontally around a slab edge or slab tear, producing trench-parallel flow beneath the slab and focused trench-normal flow in the mantle wedge beneath the arc and back-arc regions (Supplementary Fig. S1b). The two models are highly simplified, and the actual flow field in any subduction zone may reflect a combination of the two (Long, 2016). However, the detailed processes of the 3-D mantle flow field in most subduction zones are still unclear. The Cascadia subduction zone is located in western North America ranging from southern Vancouver Island to northern California (Fig. 1), where young and warm oceanic plates are subducting beneath the North American plate (e.g., Trehu et al., 1994; Krueger and Wirth, 2017). The oceanic plates include three segments: the Juan de Fuca (JdF) plate (≤10 Ma old) in the central part, and the Gorda plate to its south and the Explorer plate to its north, both of which are younger than the JdF plate (Fig. 1). The Pacific and Explorer plates bound this convergent margin to the south and north, respectively (e.g., McCaffrey et al., 2007). The subduction rate of the oceanic plates changes from 3.0 cm/year in the south to 4.5 cm/year in the north (e.g., Wilson, 1993; Long, 2016). Quaternary volcanoes exist along the Cascade Range, exhibiting different compositions of primitive basalts (e.g., Schmidt et al., 2008; * Corresponding authors at: Department of Geophysics, Graduate School of Science, Tohoku

chunks.title: Anisotropic tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone
chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: oceanic plates changes from 3.0 cm/year in the south to 4.5 cm/year in the north (e.g., Wilson, 1993; Long, 2016). Quaternary volcanoes exist along the Cascade Range, exhibiting different compositions of primitive basalts (e.g., Schmidt et al., 2008; * Corresponding authors at: Department of Geophysics, Graduate School of Science, Tohoku University, Sendai 980-8578, Japan. E-mail addresses: zhao@tohoku.ac.jp (D. Zhao), huayuanyuan@gig.ac.cn (Y. Hua). Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/pepi https://doi.org/10.1016/j.pepi.2021.106767 Received 12 February 2021; Received in revised form 25 June 2021; Accepted 29 June 2021 Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors 318 (2021) 106767 O’Hara et al., 2020), and the volcanoes are spatially clustered (see Figs. 1 and 2a). Earthquakes take place actively in the Strait of Georgia- Puget Sound, the northern Cascades Range, and NW California, whereas the seismicity is low in the middle part of the region (Fig. 1; McCrory et al., 2012; Bostock et al., 2019). Our knowledge of the subsurface structure of the Cascadia margin is obtained mainly from seismological investigations. To date, many workers have investigated this region using various seismological methods and with different spatial scales (for recent reviews, see Pavlis et al., 2012 and Long, 2016). P and S wave velocity (Vp, Vs) variations in the crust and upper mantle beneath western North America including the Cascadia margin have been revealed by the use of body-wave seismic tomography (e.g., Zhao et al., 2001; Ramachandran et al., 2006; Xue and Allen, 2007; Roth et al., 2008; Burdick et al., 2010; Schmandt and Humphreys, 2010; Tian and Zhao, 2012; Chen et al., 2015; Hawley et al., 2016; Bodmer et al., 2018; Hawley and Allen, 2019; Fan and Zhao, 2021). Surface-wave tomography is also applied to study the 3-D Vs structure of the crust and shallow mantle (≤120 km depth) beneath western North America (e.g., Moschetti et al., 2010; Porritt et al., 2011; Wagner et al., 2012; Shen et al., 2013; Gao, 2018; Delph et al., 2018; Janiszewski et al., 2019). These previous works have generally revealed the subducted JdF-Gorda plate as a high-velocity (high-V) zone dipping toward the east, and the high-V zone exhibits lateral variations in the amplitude of Vp and Vs anomalies in the upper mantle. At depths <200 km, the high-V zone looks continuous, suggesting that the sub­ ducted slab keeps its structural integrity (e.g., Roth et al., 2008; Long, 2016; Tauzin et

chunks.title: Anisotropic tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone
chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: toward the east, and the high-V zone exhibits lateral variations in the amplitude of Vp and Vs anomalies in the upper mantle. At depths <200 km, the high-V zone looks continuous, suggesting that the sub­ ducted slab keeps its structural integrity (e.g., Roth et al., 2008; Long, 2016; Tauzin et al., 2016). However, these tomographic models exhibit variations in the pattern and amplitude of velocity anomalies, probably because the data set, method and resolution scale are all different in the previous studies. A recent Vp tomographic model determined using data of onshore and offshore seismic experiments shows a prominent low- velocity (low-V) zone under the subducted JdF slab along the entire Cascadia margin, suggesting that the low-V feature reflects accumula­ tion of material from a weak, buoyant layer under the slab (Hawley et al., 2016). Using a similar data set, Bodmer et al. (2018) determined another Vp tomographic model that reveals along-margin segmentation in the subslab asthenosphere with significant low-V anomalies under areas with stronger interplate coupling and greater occurrence of episodic slip and tremor. They attributed the subslab low-V anomalies to independent mantle upwellings related to fragmentation processes at a diffuse plate boundary in southern Cascadia and hotspot-derived ma­ terial in northern Cascadia. These results suggest that subslab buoyancy affects the interplate coupling at the megathrust zone, which contributes to the localization of subduction zone segmentation and nucleation of megathrust earthquakes (Bodmer et al., 2018, 2020; Fan and Zhao, 2021). Seismic anisotropy is the direction-dependent nature of propagation velocities of seismic waves. It is a widely used physical property allowing us to investigate mantle flow and past and present deformation processes in the crust and mantle (e.g., Long and Becker, 2010; Krueger and Wirth, 2017). Seismic anisotropy in the upper mantle is generally attributed to lattice-preferred orientation (LPO) of olivine (e.g., Karato et al., 2008). When rocks in the upper mantle are deformed in the dislocation regime, mineral grains develop an LPO by dislocation creep, dynamic recrystallization and grain-boundary migration, resulting in seismic anisotropy on a macroscopic scale. The occurrence of seismic anisotropy is also influenced by pressure, temperature, water content, and differential stress (e.g., Jung and Karato, 2001; Katayama and Karato, 2006; Karato et al., 2008; Zhou et al., 2018). Although multiple processes can affect the formation of seismic anisotropy, the olivine fast Fig. 1. Tectonic background of the Cascadia subduction zone. The arrows and associated annotations show

chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: water content, and differential stress (e.g., Jung and Karato, 2001; Katayama and Karato, 2006; Karato et al., 2008; Zhou et al., 2018). Although multiple processes can affect the formation of seismic anisotropy, the olivine fast Fig. 1. Tectonic background of the Cascadia subduction zone. The arrows and associated annotations show the motion di­ rection and rate of the Juan de Fuca plate relative to the North American plate. The red triangles denote the Cascadia vol­ canoes. The green crosses denote seismicity. The red dots denote three hotspots (Bowie, Cobb and Anahim). The red box shows the present study region. The black dashed lines denote approximate boundaries of Northern Cascadia (NC), Central Cascadia (CC) and Southern Cascadia (SC). VI, Vancouver Is­ land; OP, Olympic Peninsula; PS, Puget Sound; CR, Cascade Range; OCR, Oregon Coast Range; WV, Willamette Valley; HLP, High Lava Plain; KM, Klamath Mountains. (For interpre­ tation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.) D. Zhao and Y. Hua Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors 318 (2021) 106767 axis tends to align with the maximum shear direction for simple mantle flow (e.g., Zhang and Karato, 1995; Long and Becker, 2010). In contrast, seismic anisotropy in the crust is related to fault orientation, rock cleavage or texture, and recrystallization (e.g., Babuska and Cara, 1991; Park et al., 2004). In the upper crust where confining pressures are low, seismic anisotropy can be caused by stress-aligned fractures filled with fluids (e.g., Crampin, 1994). In the lower crust where confining pres­ sures are higher, however, seismic anisotropy is mainly attributed to the LPO of minerals due to past or present deformation (Babuska and Cara, 1991). Lower crustal anisotropy in Cascadia may be also due to the foliation produced through metamorphism in the Siletz terrane that is composed of sediments and the oceanic crust (e.g., Park et al., 2004; Bostock and Christensen, 2012; Matharu et al., 2014; Krueger and Wirth, 2017). To date, many researchers have investigated seismic anisotropy in western North America including the Cascadia margin using various methods, such as shear-wave splitting (SWS) measurements (see Savage, 2002 and Long, 2016 for detailed reviews of many SWS studies), body- wave anisotropic tomography (Hearn, 1996; Buehler and Shearer, 2014; Huang and Zhao, 2013), surface-wave tomography (e.g., Wagner et al., 2013; Wagner and Long, 2013), receiver functions (e.g., Park et al., 2004;

chunks.title: Anisotropic tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone
chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: as shear-wave splitting (SWS) measurements (see Savage, 2002 and Long, 2016 for detailed reviews of many SWS studies), body- wave anisotropic tomography (Hearn, 1996; Buehler and Shearer, 2014; Huang and Zhao, 2013), surface-wave tomography (e.g., Wagner et al., 2013; Wagner and Long, 2013), receiver functions (e.g., Park et al., 2004; Nikulin et al., 2009; Krueger and Wirth, 2017), and Love-to- Rayleigh wave scattering (Rieger and Park, 2010). SWS measurements are the most popular technique to detect seismic anisotropy, but they have poor depth resolution (e.g., Savage, 2002; Liu and Gao, 2013). Other seismic methods can reveal anisotropic features at different depths, but the obtained results show relatively low consistency for the depth-dependent azimuthal anisotropy in Cascadia, probably because of their different methodology and sensitivity (e.g., Long, 2016; Zhou et al., 2018). In the past decade, the method of Vp anisotropic tomography has been successfully developed and applied to many regions, leading to meaningful results on 3-D seismic anisotropy and mantle dynamics in different tectonic settings, such as subduction zones in particular (for a detailed review, see Zhao et al., 2016). More recent applications of this method are made to, e.g., Japan (Liu and Zhao, 2017a, 2017b; Ishise et al., 2018), Alps (Hua et al., 2017), Southern California (Yu and Zhao, 2018), the Philippines (Fan and Zhao, 2019), Chile (Huang et al., 2019), Alaska (Gou et al., 2019), the Mediterranean and Middle East (Wei et al., 2019), and Antarctica (Zhang et al., 2020). Although many previous studies have investigated the isotropic Vp structure and azimuthal anisotropy of Cascadia, as mentioned above, few have jointly investi­ gated them, and none have investigated the radial component of anisotropy. Here we present the first 3-D Vp radial and azimuthal anisotropy models down to ~300 km depth beneath Cascadia, which are obtained by inverting a large number of travel-time data of local earthquakes and teleseismic events. Our results reveal detailed 3-D variations of Vp anisotropy in the crust, mantle wedge, the subducting JdF slab, and the subslab asthenosphere, which provide new constraints on the subslab mantle flow, possible hotspot-related upwelling in northern Cascadia, preserved paleo fabrics in the subducting slab, and crustal anisotropy induced by along-margin compression. 2. Data We use abundant arrival-time data of both local earthquakes (Fig. 2a) and teleseismic events (Fig. 3). The local earthquake data are selected from: (1) P and S wave arrival times of local events recorded at the

chunks.title: Anisotropic tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone
chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: fabrics in the subducting slab, and crustal anisotropy induced by along-margin compression. 2. Data We use abundant arrival-time data of both local earthquakes (Fig. 2a) and teleseismic events (Fig. 3). The local earthquake data are selected from: (1) P and S wave arrival times of local events recorded at the USArray stations (Fig. 2b); (2) hypocentral parameters and phase data of local events compiled by the Northern California Earthquake Data Center; and (3) Bulletins of the International Seismological Center (ISC) that include data contributed by several seismic networks in western North America, including the Pacific Northwest Seismic Network and the Canadian seismic network (Fig. 2b). These local earthquake data are integrated, and duplicated ones are removed, and then the local events recorded at over six seismic stations in the study region (Fig. 2) are selected, following the procedure of previous studies (Zhao et al., 2001; Tian and Zhao, 2012; Chen et al., 2015). To make the Fig. 2. (a) Epicentral distribution of local earthquakes (colour dots) used in this study. The colors denote focal depths whose scale is shown at the bottom. The black triangles show the Cascadia volcanoes. (b) Distribution of permanent and tem­ porary stations (colour squares) of different seismic networks. TA, USArray (blue); UW, the University of Washington network (red); NC, the Northern California network (green); and other network or portable stations (yellow). (For inter­ pretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.) D. Zhao and Y. Hua Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors 318 (2021) 106767 selected events distribute as uniformly as possible in the study volume, we adopted an event selection scheme (Zhao et al., 2001) to divide the study volume into small cubic blocks with a size of 10 km in the lateral direction and 5 km in depth. Among the events within each block, we select only one event that has the maximum number of arrival-time data and the smallest uncertainty in hypocentral parameters. As a result, we collected ~40,300 P-wave arrivals and ~ 9100 S-wave arrivals of 1883 local events (Fig. 2a) recorded at 350 seismic stations in the study region (Fig. 2b). The picking accuracy is ~0.1 s for P-wave arrivals and 0.1–0.2 s for S-wave arrivals (Tian and Zhao, 2012; Chen et al., 2015). A majority of the events occurred in NW California and the Strait

chunks.title: Anisotropic tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone
chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: local events (Fig. 2a) recorded at 350 seismic stations in the study region (Fig. 2b). The picking accuracy is ~0.1 s for P-wave arrivals and 0.1–0.2 s for S-wave arrivals (Tian and Zhao, 2012; Chen et al., 2015). A majority of the events occurred in NW California and the Strait of Georgia-Puget Sound, and only a small number of events took place in western Oregon (Fig. 2a). The events mainly occurred at depths <60 km, whereas some events took place at depths of 70–100 km. Most of the selected local events have hypocentral mislocations <4 km. Our teleseismic data are P-wave arrival times of distant earthquakes recorded at the USArray portable stations in the present study region (Fig. 2b). The selection of teleseismic events is based on the following criteria: (1) the events have a magnitude (M) ≥4.5; (2) the events were recorded at ten or more stations in the study region (Fig. 2b); and (3) the events have epicentral distances of 30◦-100◦so that the deepest (or turning) point of each P-wave ray path is located in the lower mantle (Zhao et al., 1994). As a result, ~105,400 P-wave arrivals from 6361 teleseismic events are collected (Fig. 3). These P-wave arrival times have a picking accuracy of ~0.1 s (Tian and Zhao, 2012; Chen et al., 2015). We obtained hypocentral parameters of the distant earthquakes from the reprocessed ISC catalog, and uncertainties of their hypocentral locations are generally <5 km (Engdahl et al., 2020). To use the teleseismic data in a tomographic inversion, we need to first determine P-wave relative travel-time residuals of each teleseismic event by removing the average travel-time residual of the event from its raw travel-time residuals at the stations in the study region (e.g., Aki et al., 1977; Zhao et al., 1994). Fig. 4 shows the distribution of relative P- wave travel-time residuals of the teleseismic events used in this study (Fig. 3), which are obtained by using the 1-D velocity model shown in Fig. 5. The teleseismic relative residuals (Fig. 4) are considered to reflect 3-D Vp variations in the crust and upper mantle under the study region, because effects of structural heterogeneities outside the study volume, i. e., those in the crust and upper mantle near the teleseismic hypocenters and those in the lower mantle, can be mostly erased by removing the average travel-time residual of each event from its raw residuals (Zhao et

chunks.title: Anisotropic tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone
chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: the study region, because effects of structural heterogeneities outside the study volume, i. e., those in the crust and upper mantle near the teleseismic hypocenters and those in the lower mantle, can be mostly erased by removing the average travel-time residual of each event from its raw residuals (Zhao et al., 1994, 2013). Note that, in the Earth’s interior, strong structural heterogeneities mainly exist in the crust and upper mantle (0–660 km depths), whereas the lower mantle is relatively homogeneous, except for the D′′ layer above the core-mantle boundary (e.g., Lay and Garnero, 2011; Zhao, 2015). This approach of teleseismic tomography has been successfully applied to many regions in the past four decades. For detailed reviews of its methodology and applications, see Zhao et al. (2013) and Zhao (2015). Figs. 6 and 7 show the distribution of the local and teleseismic rays in the study volume. It is clear that the teleseismic rays crisscross down to a depth of ~300 km. At depths of 0–90 km, both local and teleseismic rays exist and crisscross beneath the Cascadia forearc region. 3. Azimuthal anisotropy tomography Many researchers have investigated seismic velocity in media with weak azimuthal anisotropy (AAN) having a horizontal axis of hexagonal symmetry (e.g., Backus, 1965; Hearn, 1996; Eberhart-Phillips and Henderson, 2004). They found that P-wave slowness (i.e., 1/Vp) could be represented approximately as follows: S(∅) = S0(1 + Acos(2∅) + Bsin(2∅) ) (1) where S is the total slowness, S0 is the isotropic component of slowness, A and B are two AAN parameters, and ∅ is the azimuth of a ray path. The AAN fast-velocity direction (FVD) and its amplitude are expressed as follows: ψ = ⎧ ⎪ ⎪ ⎪ ⎪ ⎪ ⎪ ⎪ ⎪ ⎨ ⎪ ⎪ ⎪ ⎪ ⎪ ⎪ ⎪ ⎪ ⎩ 2tan−1 ) + ⎧ ⎨ ⎩ π 2, A > 0 0, A < 0, −π 4, A = 0, B > 0, π 4, A = 0, B < 0, α = Vfast−Vslow = ̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅ √ (2) where V0 is average isotropic velocity; Vslow and Vfast are Vp values in the slow and fast directions, respectively. Using the method of Vp anisotropic tomography (Wang and Zhao, 2008; Wang and Zhao, 2019a) that was modified from the isotropic tomography method of Zhao et al. (1992, 1994), we conducted a simultaneous inversion of the arrival times of local earthquakes and relative travel-time residuals of teleseismic

chunks.text: fast directions, respectively. Using the method of Vp anisotropic tomography (Wang and Zhao, 2008; Wang and Zhao, 2019a) that was modified from the isotropic tomography method of Zhao et al. (1992, 1994), we conducted a simultaneous inversion of the arrival times of local earthquakes and relative travel-time residuals of teleseismic events for a 3-D model of Vp azimuthal anisotropy beneath Cascadia. To express the 3-D isotropic Vp structure, we adopt a dense 3-D grid with a lateral grid interval of 0.33◦ and arrange 2-D grid meshes at depths of 10, 25, 40, 60, 90, 120, 150, 200, 250, 300, 400, 500, 600 and 700 km in the study volume. Vp perturbations at the grid nodes from the starting 1-D velocity model (Fig. 5) are taken as unknown parameters. The Vp perturbation at any point in the study volume is computed by linearly interpolating the Vp perturbations at the eight grid nodes adjacent to that point. Because better ray coverage is required to retrieve Vp anisotropy (Wang and Zhao, 2008; Huang et al., 2015), we tested different grid intervals and adopt a coarse 3-D grid with a lateral grid interval of 1◦to express Vp AAN and arrange 2-D grid meshes at depths of 20, 40, 70, 100, 250, 400, 550 and 700 km in the study volume. The isotropic Vp parameters at the dense 3-D grid nodes and the AAN parameters (A, B) at the coarse 3-D grid nodes, as well as hypocentral parameters of local events, are determined by a joint inversion of the travel-time data from the local and teleseismic events (Figs. 2–4). Hua Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors 318 (2021) 106767 and travel times, an effective 3-D ray-tracing scheme (Zhao et al., 1992) is adopted that works for a general velocity structure including curved velocity discontinuities and 3-D velocity variations everywhere in the study volume. To solve the great but sparse system of observation equations, we use a limited-memory Broyden–­ Fletcher–Goldfarb–Shanno (L-BFGS-B) algorithm for bound constrained optimization (Zhu et al., 1997; Morales and Nocedal, 2011), instead of the LSQR algorithm (Paige and Saunders, 1982) adopted by most pre­ vious tomographic studies (e.g., Zhao et al., 1992, 1994; Wang and Zhao, 2008, 2013; Zhao, 2021). This L-BFGS-B inversion scheme does not require damping regularization as the LSQR algorithm does, hence the inversion results are less influenced by human subjectivity (for de­ tails, see Wang and Zhao, 2019a, 2019b). In the starting 1-D velocity model (Fig. 5), Vp and Vs in the upper crust are 6.0 and 3.5 km/s, and in the lower crust they are 6.7 and 3.8 km/s, respectively. The iasp91 model (Kennett and Engdahl, 1991) is used for the mantle velocity. Geometries of the Moho discontinuity and the subducting JdF slab (Fig. 8) are considered in the starting model. Many tomographic studies of subduction zones including Cascadia have shown that taking into account geometries of the Moho and the sub­ ducting slab leads to a better 3-D velocity model, because theoretical travel times and ray paths can be computed more precisely, and the degree of nonlinearity of the complex tomographic problem can be greatly reduced (see Zhao et al., 1992; Chen et al., 2015 and Zhao, 2015 for detailed reviews on this issue). By taking into account the previous tomographic results of the Cas­ cadia margin (Chen et al., 2015), the slab thickness and Vp anomaly (dVp) in the starting model are taken to be 40 km and 1%, respectively, and the resulting model of Vp AAN tomography is shown in Figs. 9–11. To better visualize the AAN results, the obtained FVDs at

chunks.title: Anisotropic tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone
chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: Cas­ cadia margin (Chen et al., 2015), the slab thickness and Vp anomaly (dVp) in the starting model are taken to be 40 km and 1%, respectively, and the resulting model of Vp AAN tomography is shown in Figs. 9–11. To better visualize the AAN results, the obtained FVDs at the coarse 3-D grid nodes are interpolated (Figs. 9 and 11). To investigate the effect of the assigned slab parameters (i.e., slab thickness and dVp) on the final tomographic result, we conducted two more inversions using different starting models including the JdF slab. In one, we set the slab thickness to 60 km and the slab dVp to 1%; in the other, the slab thickness is 40 km and the slab dVp is 2%. The obtained results (Supplementary Figs. S2 and S3) show that very similar 3-D Vp and AAN images are obtained. The correlation coefficient between our preferred model (Fig. 9) and each of the two models (Supplementary Figs. S2 and S3) is 0.91 and 0.92, respectively. We also conducted a simultaneous inversion of the local and tele­ seismic data (Figs. 2-4) for only isotropic Vp tomography without considering Vp anisotropy, and the obtained Vp model is shown in Supplementary Figs. S4 and S5. In all these tomographic images shown in this paper and the Supporting Information, the isotropic Vp pertur­ bations are relative to the 1-D velocity model shown in Fig. 5. The general pattern of the isotropic Vp images (Supplementary Figs. S4 and S5) is similar to that determined by the anisotropic inversion (Figs. 9 and 10), and the correlation coefficient between the two models is 0.71. Significant Vp variations of up to 5% are revealed (Figs. 9 and 10). The subducting JdF slab is imaged as a high-V zone down to ~150 km depth, and intermediate-depth earthquakes occurred within the slab (Fig. 10). Prominent low-V zones exist in the crust and mantle wedge beneath the Cascadia volcanoes and the back-arc. Low-V zones are also visible in the mantle wedge beneath the forearc (Fig. 10). Broad low-V anomalies appear in the asthenosphere under the subducting JdF slab (Figs. 9 and 10). Below the forearc region, the subslab low-V zones look more significant under northern and southern Cascadia, but they have a lower amplitude under central Cascadia (Fig. 9). The subslab low-V anomaly becomes predominent under the volcanic front in central Cascadia (Fig. 10J). This feature also accords

chunks.title: Anisotropic tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone
chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: (Figs. 9 and 10). Below the forearc region, the subslab low-V zones look more significant under northern and southern Cascadia, but they have a lower amplitude under central Cascadia (Fig. 9). The subslab low-V anomaly becomes predominent under the volcanic front in central Cascadia (Fig. 10J). This feature also accords with the previous tomo­ graphic models of the study region (e.g., Chen et al., 2015; Hawley et al., 2016; Bodmer et al., 2018, 2020). The anisotropic features exhibit strong lateral variations and also change with depth in the crust and upper mantle (Figs. 9 and 11), which are described as follows. In the crust and uppermost mantle (0–40 km depths), the FVD pat­ terns at different depths are generally consistent: they are oriented NW- SE in northern Cascadia, N-S to NNE-SSW in central Cascadia, and NE- SW to E-W in southern Cascadia. However, the FVDs exhibit strong depth variations beneath southern Cascadia where the Mendocino Triple Junction is located and so the structure and tectonics are complex (e.g., Verdonck and Zandt, 1994; Furlong and Schwartz, 2004): at 10 km depth, the FVDs change from NNE-SSW and N-S in the west to WNW-ESE in the east; at 25 km depth, they change from NE-SW in the west to WNW-ESE in the east; at 40 km depth, the FVDs are generally NE-SW in the west but roughly E-W in the east. In this depth range (0–40 km), the FVDs also exhibit lateral variations from the west to the east: they are roughly margin-parallel or N-S under the forearc, but change to roughly E-W under the Cascadia volcanoes; east of the volcanoes, the FVDs are generally E-W under northern Cascadia but change to N-S beneath central Cascadia (Fig. 9). In the upper mantle (60–150 km depths), the FVDs exhibit a clear and consistent pattern (Fig. 9). In northern Cascadia where a strong low- V zone exists, the FVDs are generally N-S, though at 60 km depth they become WNW-ESE or nearly E-W near the volcanic front and back-arc area. In central Cascadia, the FVDs are generally NNE-SSW to NE-SE under the forearc but become N-S in the volcanic front and back-arc area. In southern Cascadia where a strong low-V anomaly exists, the FVDs are NE-SW but become N-S at the eastern edge of the study region. In the subslab asthenosphere, the FVD pattern is also very clear and consistent at depth. In the

chunks.title: Anisotropic tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone
chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: become N-S in the volcanic front and back-arc area. In southern Cascadia where a strong low-V anomaly exists, the FVDs are NE-SW but become N-S at the eastern edge of the study region. In the subslab asthenosphere, the FVD pattern is also very clear and consistent at depth. In the easternmost part (back-arc) of the study re­ gion (Fig. 9), the FVDs are generally N-S. In most of the study region, the FVDs are NE-SW in northern Cascadia, E-W in central Cascadia, and NE- SW to NNE-SSW in southern Cascadia. To investigate the reliability of our AAN tomography (Figs. 9–11), we performed detailed synthetic tests. We also investigated the possible trade-off between the isotropic Vp structure and azimuthal anisotropy. These test results are shown in Supplementary Figs. S6–S15 and they are Fig. 5. Hua Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors 318 (2021) 106767 discussed in detail in the online Supporting Information and Section 5.1. These tests suggest that the lateral resolution is ~0.33◦for the isotropic Vp image and ~ 1◦for the AAN result. The input FVD patterns are generally well resolved, indicating that the main AAN features as mentioned above are generally reliable. 4. Radial anisotropy tomography Using the same data set of local and teleseismic events (Figs. 2–4), we also determine 3-D Vp radial anisotropy (RAN) tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone by applying the method of Wang and Zhao (2013) that was also modified from Zhao et al. (1992, 1994). However, similar to the AAN tomography as described above, the inversion is conducted using the L-BFGS-B scheme instead of the LSQR algorithm adopted by Wang and Zhao (2013). This approach of RAN tomographic inversion is based on an assumption of weak RAN with a vertical axis of hexagonal symmetry. P-wave slowness (S = 1/Vp) in the RAN medium is expressed as S = S0[1 + Mcos(2i) ] (3) where S0 is isotropic component of slowness, i is ray incident angle, and M is a RAN parameter. Similar to the above-mentioned AAN tomography, we adopt a coarse 3-D grid with a lateral grid interval of 1◦to represent the Vp RAN structure and arrange 2-D grid meshes at depths of 20, 40, 70, 100, 250, 400, 550 and 700 km in the study volume. A dense 3-D grid with a lateral grid interval of 0.33◦is adopted to represent the 3-D isotropic Vp structure, which is the same as that for the AAN inversion. The RAN parameters (M) at the coarse 3-D grid nodes and the isotropic Vp pa­ rameters at the dense 3-D grid nodes, as well as hypocentral parameters of local events, are determined simultaneously by inverting the local and teleseismic data (Figs. 2–4). Figs. 12 and 13 show map views and vertical

chunks.text: RAN parameters (M) at the coarse 3-D grid nodes and the isotropic Vp pa­ rameters at the dense 3-D grid nodes, as well as hypocentral parameters of local events, are determined simultaneously by inverting the local and teleseismic data (Figs. 2–4). Figs. 12 and 13 show map views and vertical cross-sections of the obtained Vp RAN tomography of the study region. To better visualize the images, the obtained RAN results at the coarse 3-D grid nodes are interpolated (Figs. 12 and 13). The pattern of the isotropic Vp image is similar to those determined by the isotropic inversion (Supplementary Figs. S4 and S5) and the AAN inversion (Figs. 9 and 10), but there are some differences between them. For example, the spatial extent and amplitude of the subslab low-V zones exhibit some changes in the three Vp models. The RAN features exhibit strong lateral variations and also change with depth in the study volume (Figs. 12 and 13). The isotropic Vp images at 25 and 40 km depths obtained by the AAN inversion (Fig. 9) differ from those obtained by the other two inversions (Figs. 12 and Supplementary Fig. S4), which may reflect the stronger and more complex seismic anisotropies at those depths due to strong interactions of the subducting oceanic slab and the overlying continental plate in and around the megathrust zone. In the upper and lower crust (at 10 and 25 km depths in Fig. 12), the RAN results exhibit considerable lateral variations. In northern and central Cascadia, positive RAN is predominant, whereas negative RAN appears in the northeastern part where the Cascadia volcanoes exist. In southern Cascadia, negative RAN prevails under the forearc, whereas positive RAN is predominant beneath the volcanic front and back-arc areas. In the subducting JdF slab, positive RAN prevails under the forearc except for the northern edge of the study region and in deeper areas (> ~90 km depth) (Figs. 12 and 13). In the mantle wedge above the sub­ ducted JdF slab and beneath the Cascadia volcanoes in particular, negative RAN is predominant (Fig. 13). In the asthenosphere below the subducting JdF slab, positive RAN prevails under the forearc (see Fig. 13I), whereas negative RAN is pre­ dominant under the Cascadia volcanoes and the back-arc region (Fig. 13J). We made extensive resolution analyses to examine the robustness of the RAN tomographic model (Supplementary Figs. S16–S27), similar to those for the AAN tomography

We made extensive resolution analyses to examine the robustness of the RAN tomographic model (Supplementary Figs. S16–S27), similar to those for the AAN tomography as mentioned above (Supplementary Figs. S6–S15). The results of these synthetic tests (Supplementary Figs. S16–S27) suggest that the lateral resolution is ~0.33◦for the isotropic Vp image and ~1◦for the RAN image. The input RAN patterns are generally well reconstructed, indicating that the main RAN features are robust. For details of the test results and related discussions, see the Supporting Information. As described above, using the same data set of local and teleseismic events (Figs. 2–4), we obtained three Vp tomographic models of the Cascadia margin. The root-mean-square (RMS) travel-time residual of our data set is 0.370 s for the starting 1-D velocity model (Fig. 5). The RMS residual is reduced to 0.324 s for the 3-D isotropic Vp model (Supplementary Figs. S4 and S5), to 0.322 s for the 3-D AAN tomo­ graphic model (Figs. 9–11), and to 0.316 s for the 3-D RAN tomographic Fig. 8. The variance reductions from the starting 1-D Vp model to the 3-D isotropic Vp model, the AAN model and the RAN model are 30.4%, 32.0% and 37.2%, respectively. However, the RMS residuals for the final three Vp models are all greater than the picking errors of the P-wave arrival-time data (~0.1 s). We consider that the degree of data misfit is mainly caused by uncertainties of the hypocen­ tral parameters of the local events, as well as smaller-scale velocity anomalies in the study volume than the spatial resolution of the tomo­ graphic models. Note that the RMS

chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: P-wave arrival-time data (~0.1 s). We consider that the degree of data misfit is mainly caused by uncertainties of the hypocen­ tral parameters of the local events, as well as smaller-scale velocity anomalies in the study volume than the spatial resolution of the tomo­ graphic models. Note that the RMS residual for the 3-D RAN model (0.316 s) is smaller than that for the 3-D AAN model (0.322 s), although there are a smaller number of anisotropic parameters for the RAN model than those for the AAN model, indicating that our data set is better fitted by the 3-D RAN model. This result may suggest that the RAN assumption works better than the AAN assumption for the present study region and our data set. In other words, RAN may be more significant than AAN in the study volume (perhaps mainly in the subslab mantle that occupies most of the study volume), and/or our data set is more sensitive to RAN than AAN due to the ray coverage and crisscrossing in the study volume (Figs. 6 and 7). To clarify this issue, we will need a better data set including ray paths that have a better distribution in both azimuth and incident angle. 5. Discussion 5.1. Isotropic versus anisotropic inversions To know whether the reductions of the RMS travel-time residual from the isotropic Vp inversion to the anisotropic inversions are statis­ tically significant or not, we conducted the so-called F test (see the Supporting Information for details). The F test results show that the RMS residual reductions from the isotropic tomographic inversion to the AAN and RAN inversions are indeed statistically significant, indicating that our data set is certainly better fitted by the two anisotropic Vp models (AAN and RAN) than the isotropic Vp model. Theoretically, it is possible to invert for the isotropic Vp, AAN and RAN structures simultaneously (e.g., Liu and Zhao, 2017b). However, this joint inversion approach requires excellent 3-D ray coverage in both azimuth and incident angle in a study volume that is covered by a very dense seismic network. In a practical application of this approach to the Japan subduction zone where over 2000 seismic stations are in opera­ tion, however, the joint inversion for both AAN and RAN is not very stable due to the still imperfect ray coverage there, though over half a million P-wave rays from teleseismic events and local rays from shallow

chunks.title: Anisotropic tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone
chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: to the Japan subduction zone where over 2000 seismic stations are in opera­ tion, however, the joint inversion for both AAN and RAN is not very stable due to the still imperfect ray coverage there, though over half a million P-wave rays from teleseismic events and local rays from shallow and deep earthquakes in the subducted Philippine Sea and Pacific slabs beneath Japan were used (Liu and Zhao, 2017b). Hence, to obtain stable and reliable inversion results, in this work we conduct the anisotropic tomographic inversions separately. That is, we first conduct an inversion for the 3-D isotropic Vp and AAN structures, then we perform an inversion for the 3-D isotropic Vp and RAN structures of the Cascadia Fig. 9. Hua Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors 318 (2021) 106767 margin. As a result, we have obtained stable results of AAN and RAN tomography of the study region, as shown in the last two sections. However, it should be noted that direct comparison of the AAN and RAN models should be done carefully for the following two reasons. (1) The two models are independently derived and it is unclear what the trade-offs are between the AAN and RAN parameters. Their trade-offs may be due to a dip in the symmetry axis (i.e., apparent anisotropy), particularly in and around the dipping subducting slab. It would be better to take into account dipping symmetry axes in the inversion in a future work. (2) The resulting isotropic Vp images exhibit some differ­ ences, suggesting that the interplay between the isotropic and aniso­ tropic structures is also unknown, partly due to the imperfect ray coverage (e.g., Huang et al., 2015). As shown in the

chunks.title: Anisotropic tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone
chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: dipping symmetry axes in the inversion in a future work. (2) The resulting isotropic Vp images exhibit some differ­ ences, suggesting that the interplay between the isotropic and aniso­ tropic structures is also unknown, partly due to the imperfect ray coverage (e.g., Huang et al., 2015). As shown in the Supporting Infor­ mation, in the isotropic inversion, the effects of AAN and RAN are mapped into the isotropic Vp image. The inhomogeneity of angular coverage of ray paths may produce an apparent anisotropy (Ishise et al., 2018). In the AAN and RAN inversions, the contributions of seismic anisotropy to the travel-time residuals are separated, at least partially, from the isotropic Vp image. As a result, the three isotropic Vp models exhibit some differences in the amplitude and spatial extent of Vp anomalies and, in particular, in the subslab mantle. These differences in the isotropic Vp image may reflect the greater effect of seismic anisot­ ropy on the subslab mantle where stronger mantle flow is suggested to exist (Chen et al., 2015; Hawley et al., 2016; Bodmer et al., 2018). Because the effect of anisotropy is removed, at least partly, by the AAN and RAN inversions, the isotropic Vp image of the subslab mantle may be better recovered in Figs. 9, 10, 12 and 13. Low-V zones are also visible in the mantle wedge beneath the Cascadia forearc (Figs. 10 and 13), which reflect serpenti­ nization of the forearc mantle related to abundant fluids from dehydration reactions of the young and warm JdF slab (e.g., Zhao et al., 2001; Hyndman and Peacock, 2003; Chen et al., 2015). These features have been revealed in almost all subduction zones by isotropic tomo­ graphic inversions in the past three to four decades (e.g., Zhao, 2021). Our present results and other recent studies show that these low-V features remain even after performing anisotropic tomographic in­ versions, indicating that they are certainly well-established common Fig. 12. Hua Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors 318 (2021) 106767 features and processes of subduction zones (e.g., Zhao et al., 2016; Liu and Zhao, 2017b; Gou et al., 2019; Fan and Zhao, 2019; Huang et al., 2019). As described above, our results show that the Cascade subduction zone is characterized by regional variations in both isotropic and anisotropic Vp structures between northern, central and southern parts of the region. Three isolated low-Vp anomalies are visible in northern, central and southern Cascadia at depths of 0–40 km beneath the Cas­ cadia forearc (Figs. 10 and 13). Delph et al. (2018) revealed significant north-south Vs variations in the crust and uppermost mantle under the Cascadia forearc due to different amounts of fluid from the slab dehy­ dration: low-Vs anomalies appear in northern and southern Cascadia but not in central Cascadia where the Siletzia terrane extends to the slab interface. Vp and Vs have different sensitivities to fluid and compositional variations, which may have caused, at least partly, the differences between our Vp image and the Vs image of Delph et al. (2018). 5.2. Azimuthal anisotropy and lateral mantle flow Azimuthal anisotropy (AAN) of the Cascadia margin has been investigated by many workers using different methods (see Savage, 2002 and Long, 2016 for detailed reviews). Although the obtained re­ sults show relatively low consistency for the depth-varying azimuthal anisotropy, probably because of their different methodology and sensi­ tivity (e.g., Long, 2016; Zhou et al., 2018), the previous studies have revealed anisotropic features at certain depths of the Cascadia margin, which reflect some geodynamic processes of this typical subduction Fig. 13. Horizontal and vertical bars represent positive and negative RAN results, respectively. Scales for the isotropic Vp perturbation and the RAN amplitude are shown on the right. Other labels are the same as those in Fig. 10. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.) D. Zhao and Y. Hua Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors 318 (2021) 106767 zone. Seismic anisotropy in the Cascadia crust and uppermost

chunks.title: Anisotropic tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone
chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: those in Fig. 10. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.) D. Zhao and Y. Hua Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors 318 (2021) 106767 zone. Seismic anisotropy in the Cascadia crust and uppermost mantle has been investigated using shear-wave splitting (SWS) measurements from local earthquakes in the crust and the subducted JdF slab down to ~80 km depth, as well as tectonic tremors that occurred around the Moho depth along the slab interface (Fig. 14a–c). The local SWS results show that the fast S-wave polarization direction is generally trench- parallel. Pn-wave anisotropic tomography shows that the fast Vp di­ rection in the uppermost mantle beneath the Cascadia forearc is generally margin-parallel (Hearn, 1996; Buehler and Shearer, 2014). Our Vp AAN results are well consistent with the local SWS and Pn to­ mography results, showing trench-parallel FVDs in the crust and up­ permost mantle (Fig. 14d), which may reflect along-margin compression in Cascadia (Wang et al., 1995; Currie et al., 2001; Matharu et al., 2014; Huesca-P´erez et al., 2017). A few local sites in northern Cascadia exhibit trench-oblique fast SWS directions (Fig. 14a,b), which may be areas with strong coupling between the subducting JdF plate and the overlying North American plate (Cassidy and Bostock, 1996; Currie et al., 2001; Balfour et al., 2012; Bostock and Christensen, 2012). However, our Vp AAN tomography cannot reveal those local sites due to the lower lateral resolution (~30 km) of our model compared to the local SWS mea­ surements that are made at individual seismic stations. Wagner et al. (2013) estimated fast directions of Rayleigh-wave phase velocities of the Cascadia margin at different periods. They sug­ gested that anisotropic material exists within or directly above the subducting JdF slab. Fig. 15 shows a comparison of the fast directions of Rayleigh-wave phase velocities at periods of 33, 50, 66 and 77 s with FVDs of our Vp azimuthal anisotropy at corresponding depths of 50, 75, 90 and 110 km. The two results are similar in broad areas at the longer periods (50–77 s) and greater depths (75–110 km) (Fig. 15b–d), but they are different at the shorter period (33 s) and 50 km depth (Fig. 15a). The model of Wagner et al. (2013) shows azimuthal variations of Rayleigh- wave phase velocity at different periods and is not a 3-D

chunks.title: Anisotropic tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone
chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: longer periods (50–77 s) and greater depths (75–110 km) (Fig. 15b–d), but they are different at the shorter period (33 s) and 50 km depth (Fig. 15a). The model of Wagner et al. (2013) shows azimuthal variations of Rayleigh- wave phase velocity at different periods and is not a 3-D Vs AAN model, hence it may not be directly comparable with our Vp AAN model. Despite these differences, the two models show some similarities (Fig. 15) and suggest that hydrated outer-rise faults in the upper part of the slab may result in the azimuthal anisotropy (e.g., Faccenda et al., 2008; Liu and Zhao, 2017a). As mentioned above, two simplified endmember models have been proposed for the mantle flow field in subduction zones: one is the 2-D entrained flow model (Supplementary Fig. S1a), the other is the 3-D toroidal flow model (Supplementary Fig. S1b). SWS measurements of teleseismic SKS waves have revealed large splitting times of ~1 to 2 s with fast directions of NE-SW to ENE-WSW in the Cascadia margin (see Fig. 16a–d), which are interpreted by using the 2-D entrained flow model, i.e., reflecting mantle flow in the subslab asthenosphere that is entrained by the subducting JdF slab (e.g., Savage, 2002; Eakin et al., 2010; Liu et al., 2014; Bodmer et al., 2015; Martin-Short et al., 2015; Long, 2016). However, the SWS results have low depth resolution because SWS is an integrated effect of seismic anisotropy (AAN) along seismic ray paths (Savage, 2002; Long, 2016). Our results of Vp AAN show that the FVDs are generally NE-SW to nearly E-W in the subslab mantle beneath central and southern Cascadia (Fig. 16e), which may reflect 2-D entrained flow in the subslab mantle under central and southern Cascadia. However, there is a depth-dependence in the FVDs in the subslab mantle under the central part of the subduction zone. The different patterns of anisotropic distribution in central and southern Cascadia may suggest regional features of the slab geometry and flow Fig. 14. (2017). Orientations of the black and colour bars denote fast polarization directions, which reflect shear-wave azimuthal anisotropy in the crust and uppermost mantle beneath the Cascadia margin. Hua Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors 318 (2021) 106767 pattern. Using the technique of Wei et al. (2019), we estimate synthetic SKS splitting parameters in the Cascadia margin from our 3-D Vp AAN model (Fig. 9). Fig. 17 shows a comparison of the synthetic SKS splitting pa­ rameters and the observations (Bodmer et al., 2015 as shown in Fig. 16a). The fast directions of the synthetic and observed SKS results are generally consistent in the Cascadia margin, except for the area in and around Washington State where strong lateral variations in Vp RAN exist in the subducting slab and the subslab mantle (Fig. 13a–c). In northern Cascadia, our results show roughly N-S FVDs in the subslab mantle at depths of 90–150 km (Fig. 9), which is a very reliable feature confirmed by our extensive resolution tests (Supplementary Figs. S6–S15). This result can be well explained by the 3-D toroidal flow model (Supplementary Fig. S1b), providing strong evidence for the ex­ istence of 3-D toroidal flow in the subslab mantle beneath northern Cascadia due to the trench migration and slab rollback (e.g., Long, 2016). A recent geochemical study of basalts from northern Cascadia revealed margin-parallel gradients in trace elements and isotope ratios that extend at least 150 km into the arc from the northern margin of the subducted JdF plate (Mullen and Weis, 2015). Southerly increases in the geochemical features indicate greater mantle depletion and higher slab- derived contributions with distance from the northern edge of the JdF slab (i.e., the Nootka fault zone; Cassidy et al., 1998). Temperatures and pressures of mantle melt segregation also decrease to the south. The gradients are interpreted as a result of slab rollback-induced toroidal flow at the northern slab edge, whereby enriched (OIB-type) astheno­ spheric mantle from below the slab is drawn into the mantle wedge in a trench-parallel southerly

chunks.title: Anisotropic tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone
chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: 1998). Temperatures and pressures of mantle melt segregation also decrease to the south. The gradients are interpreted as a result of slab rollback-induced toroidal flow at the northern slab edge, whereby enriched (OIB-type) astheno­ spheric mantle from below the slab is drawn into the mantle wedge in a trench-parallel southerly flow pattern. The trench-parallel geochemical gradients in the northern Cascadia arc (Mullen and Weis, 2015) are consistent with our Vp AAN result (Fig. 9), numerical modeling, and experimental studies (Long, 2016; Zhou et al., 2018). These results suggest that trench-parallel 3-D toroidal mantle flow may be a common phenomenon near slab edges and slab tears (Mullen and Weis, 2015). 5.3. Radial anisotropy and vertical mantle flow To the best of our knowledge, there has been no previous work of radial anisotropy (RAN) in the present study region. Therefore, here we discuss our RAN results by referring to other subduction zones where RAN tomography has been determined, such as Japan (Gou et al., 2018; Liu and Zhao, 2017b; Niu et al., 2016; Wang and Zhao, 2013), Alps (Hua et al., 2017), Alaska (Gou et al., 2019), and the Philippines (Fan and Zhao, 2019). Our RAN results (Fig. 13) show that the low-V anomalies in the mantle wedge beneath the Cascadia volcanoes generally exhibit nega­ tive RAN (i.e., Vpv > Vph). This feature has been revealed in volcanic areas of other subduction zones, such as NE Japan, SW Japan, Alaska, and the Philippines. This RAN feature is very possibly caused by hot and wet mantle upwelling and/or olivine fabric changes in the mantle wedge associated with fluids from dehydration reactions of the subducting slabs (Zhao et al., 2016; Liu and Zhao, 2017b; Zhao, 2021). Within the subducting JdF slab, positive RAN (i.e., Vpv < Vph) is predominant under the forearc region where the slab dip angle is small, whereas negative RAN occurs in some areas under the volcanic front and back-arc areas where the slab dip angle becomes larger (Fig. 13). Similar features have been found in Japan, Alps, Alaska and the Philippines, showing that positive RAN prevails in flat or gently dipping slabs, whereas negative RAN occurs in nearly vertical or steeply dipping slabs. The flat or gently dipping slabs may still keep their original anisotropy produced at the mid-ocean ridge and slightly modified at the outer-rise before subduction, and so exhibit positive RAN (e.g., Zhao et al., 2016). In contrast,

chunks.title: Anisotropic tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone
chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: dipping slabs, whereas negative RAN occurs in nearly vertical or steeply dipping slabs. The flat or gently dipping slabs may still keep their original anisotropy produced at the mid-ocean ridge and slightly modified at the outer-rise before subduction, and so exhibit positive RAN (e.g., Zhao et al., 2016). In contrast, the nearly vertical or steeply dipping slabs may be strongly deformed, either by compression or extension, by the gravity effect, and/or the slab fabrics are strongly modified due to the longer stay of the slabs in the high-temperature and high-pressure mantle environment, leading to negative RAN. Hence, the slab dip angle may play an important role in the slab RAN. The RAN in the subslab mantle exhibits the same pattern as that of the slab RAN (Figs. 12 and 13). That is, positive RAN generally occurs in both the slab and subslab mantle beneath the Cascadia forearc, whereas negative RAN generally exists in those parts under the Cascadia vol­ canoes and the back-arc where the slab becomes steeper. Our synthetic tests show that this is a reliable feature (Supplementary Figs. S24–S27). This RAN pattern in the subslab mantle may indicate that the mantle flow below the slab is formed by the entrainment of asthenosphere with the overriding slab due to strong coupling between the slab and subslab Fig. 15. The red bars denote fast Vp directions by this study at the corre­ sponding depth shown on each map. The background colors denote azimuthal difference (in degree) between the red and black bars, whose scale is shown at the bottom. The dark colors denote the azimuthal differences <30◦. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.) D. Zhao and Y. Hua Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors 318 (2021) 106767 mantle, as shown by the 2-D entrained flow model (Supplementary Fig. S1a). 5.4. Subslab flows in northern and southern Cascadia Beneath northern Cascadia, negative RAN features in the slab and subslab mantle are visible in both the forearc and back-arc (panels A and I in Fig. 13), and the subslab low-V zone at depths >170

chunks.title: Anisotropic tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone
chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: 2-D entrained flow model (Supplementary Fig. S1a). 5.4. Subslab flows in northern and southern Cascadia Beneath northern Cascadia, negative RAN features in the slab and subslab mantle are visible in both the forearc and back-arc (panels A and I in Fig. 13), and the subslab low-V zone at depths >170 km exhibits NE- SW AAN (Figs. 9 and 11). As a whole, our anisotropic results support the proposal of Bodmer et al. (2018) who attributed the subslab low-V zone to thermally buoyant mantle derived from nearby oceanic hotspots including Cobb, Bowie and Anahim (Fig. 1). Since these hotspots in the NE Pacific have small buoyancy fluxes and their ascent rates are slow (e. g., Sleep, 1990; Zhao, 2015), the anomalous mantle ascends gradually toward the Cascadia margin leading to decompression melting. Considering the JdF plate motion toward the northeast (Fig. 1), SKS splitting results consistent with the plate motion (Fig. 16a–c), and the northeastward extension of the low-V zone from the JdF Ridge to northern Cascadia, Bodmer et al. (2018) proposed that the hotspot materials are transported toward the northeast and accumulate under northern Cascadia. This mechanism can generally explain our RAN and AAN results for the subslab mantle under northern Cascadia. In the subslab low-V zone, the RAN is negative in the northern and central parts of the low-V zone, but becomes positive in its southern part (Figs. 12 and 13), suggesting that vertical and horizontal flows may coexist in the subslab low-V zone, due to the complex interactions be­ tween the subducting slab and the thermally buoyant mantle flows from the nearby hotspots. Beneath southern Cascadia, positive RAN occurs in the subslab low-V zone (Figs. 12 and 13), which exhibits NE-SW AANs (Figs. 9 and 11). Bodmer et al. (2018) attributed the subslab low-V anomaly there to mantle flow ascending toward the northwest and consequential decompression melting that extends from the subslab upward and to­ ward the Gorda deformation zone. However, this mechanism cannot explain our present results. The NE-SW AAN and positive RAN in the subslab mantle can be best explained by the entrainment of astheno­ sphere with the overriding JdF-Gorda plate that is subducting and moving toward the northeast (Fig. 1). Bodmer et al. (2015) and Martin-Short et al. (2015) show a different result of azimuthal anisotropy off southern Cascadia suggesting asthenospheric mantle flow toward the northwest beneath the offshore region, whereas our results show the

chunks.title: Anisotropic tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone
chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: with the overriding JdF-Gorda plate that is subducting and moving toward the northeast (Fig. 1). Bodmer et al. (2015) and Martin-Short et al. (2015) show a different result of azimuthal anisotropy off southern Cascadia suggesting asthenospheric mantle flow toward the northwest beneath the offshore region, whereas our results show the anisotropy beneath the land area where the JdF plate is subducting and so the mantle flow field should be more complex in the subduction zone. In addition, their offshore studies show the shear-wave polarization anisotropy and their SWS measure­ ments may suffer from poor depth resolution, whereas our results show 3-D images of Vp azimuthal anisotropy. These factors may have caused the differences between our Vp results and their SWS results. Fig. 16. SKS wave splitting measurements by (a) Bodmer et al. (2015), (b) Martin-Short et al. (2015), and (c) spatially averaged splitting parameters by Long (2016) from the database of Liu et al. (2014). (d) A cartoon showing the SKS wave splitting and its measurement (Crampin, 1994). (e) P-wave azimuthal anisotropy to­ mography at depths of 120–300 km by this study (the same as Fig. 9). D. Zhao and Y. Hua Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors 318 (2021) 106767 6. Conclusions We use abundant P-wave arrival times of local earthquakes and relative travel-time residuals of teleseismic events to determine the first P-wave tomography of 3-D azimuthal and radial anisotropy of the Cas­ cadia subduction zone. The main findings of this work are summarized as follows. (1) The fast-velocity directions (FVDs) of P-wave azimuthal anisot­ ropy in the crust are generally trench-parallel, which may reflect N-S compression along the Cascadia margin. (2) Negative radial anisotropy (RAN; i.e., Vpv > Vph) occurs in the crust and mantle wedge beneath the Cascadia volcanoes and back-arc area, reflecting hot and wet upwelling flows associated with fluids from dehydration reactions of the young and warm Juan de Fuca - Gorda plate that is subducting toward the north­ east beneath the North American plate. (3) Margin-parallel FVDs occur in the subducted slab under the Cascadia forearc. This result may suggest that the gently-dipping slab may still keep its frozen-in anisotropy formed at the mid- ocean ridge and modified at the outer-rise with trench-parallel normal faults filled with hydrous phases, though the result may be affected by other factors such as the seafloor topography, lateral variations in the sedimentary layer and oceanic crust atop

chunks.title: Anisotropic tomography of the Cascadia subduction zone
chunks.journal: Physics of the Earth and Planetary Interiors
chunks.text: slab may still keep its frozen-in anisotropy formed at the mid- ocean ridge and modified at the outer-rise with trench-parallel normal faults filled with hydrous phases, though the result may be affected by other factors such as the seafloor topography, lateral variations in the sedimentary layer and oceanic crust atop the slab, and oceanic fracture zones that are normal or oblique to the trench axis. (4) The RAN pattern in the subducted slab is nearly the same as that in the subslab mantle. That is, positive RAN occurs beneath the Cascadia forearc, whereas negative RAN appears under the Cas­ cadia volcanoes and the back-arc. This feature suggests that the subducting slab and the subslab asthenosphere are strongly coupled, and the mantle flow below the slab is formed by the entrainment of asthenosphere with the overriding slab. (5) In northern Cascadia, a prominent low-velocity (low-V) zone is revealed in the mantle below the slab, and the low-V zone ex­ hibits NE-SW FVDs and negative RAN. The subslab low-V zone may reflect thermally buoyant mantle materials from the close oceanic hotspots (Cobb, Bowie and Anahim). The hot and buoyant mantle materials are transported northeastward by the overriding slab and gradually accumulate beneath northern Cascadia, resulting in decompression melting. (6) In southern Cascadia, a subslab low-V zone is also revealed that exhibits NE-SW FVDs and positive RAN, reflecting the entrain­ ment of asthenosphere with the overriding Juan de Fuca - Gorda plate.
